feed.title: 一個在成長路上前行的人
feed.subtitle: 真正的成長，不是超越他人，而是超越昨日的自己

entry.title: BME 2104 Tissue Engineering – 5
entry.content:
BME 2104 Tissue Engineering – 5

Lecture Overview

  • 每年必出嘅八大題目 (Top 8 topics that appear every year):
    1. Tensegrity Model 張拉整體模型 🔴
    2. Mechanoreceptors 機械感受器 and Force Propagation 力傳播 🟢
    3. Integrins 整合素 and Focal Adhesions 黏著斑 🔴
    4. Integrin-Dependent Mechanosignaling 依賴整合素的機械信號轉導 🟢
    5. Effects of Cell Shape 細胞形狀 and Mechanical Forces 機械力 on Differentiation 分化 🔵
    6. Scaffold Requirements and Properties 支架要求與特性 🟢
    7. Types of Scaffolds 支架類型 (Bioinert 生物惰性, Bioactive 生物活性, Bioresorbable 生物可吸收) 🔴
    8. Scaffold Materials 支架材料 (Natural vs Synthetic 天然 vs 合成, Metals 金屬, Ceramics 陶瓷) 🔵
  • 顏色標記 (Colour code): 🔴 Memorise 必須背誦 | 🟢 Understand 必須理解 | 🔵 Apply 必須應用

Topic 1 – Tissue Engineering Paradigm 組織工程範式

大圖景 (Big Picture)

The tissue engineering paradigm integrates cells 細胞, biocompatible materials 生物相容性材料, biochemical factors 生化因子 like growth factors 生長因子, and biophysical factors 生物物理因子 such as cyclic mechanical loading 週期性機械負荷 to regenerate tissues.

思維導圖 (Mind map):
Paradigm 範式 → Cells 細胞 (source 來源) → Materials 材料 (scaffolds 支架) → Biochemical 生化因子 (growth factors 生長因子) → Biophysical 生物物理因子 (mechanical loading 機械負荷) → Outcomes 結果 (tissue regeneration 組織再生)

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • Core components 核心組成部分: cells 細胞, biocompatible materials 生物相容性材料, biochemical factors 生化因子 (e.g., growth factors), biophysical factors 生物物理因子 (e.g., cyclic mechanical loading).
  • Aims to regenerate 3D tissues using a multidisciplinary approach 多學科方法.
  • Cells 細胞 provide the building blocks for new tissue formation.
  • Materials 材料 act as scaffolds 支架 for cell attachment 細胞黏附 and growth 生長.
  • Biochemical factors 生化因子 stimulate cell proliferation 細胞增殖 and differentiation 分化.
  • Biophysical factors 生物物理因子 mimic in vivo 體內 mechanical environments to guide tissue development.
  • Paradigm emphasizes biomimicry 仿生 of natural ECM 細胞外基質.
  • Applications include hard tissues 硬組織 like bone and soft tissues 軟組織 like vascular.
  • Challenges: controlling differentiation, proliferation, and immune response 免疫反應.
  • Mechanical environment 機械環境 plays a key role in cell signaling 細胞信號傳導 and phenotype 表型.
  • Scaffolds must support nutrient diffusion 營養擴散 and vascularization 血管化.
  • Cyclic stretching 週期性拉伸 is essential for constructs like myocardium 心肌.
  • Integration of factors ensures synchronized tissue function 同步的組織功能.

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - Biochemical vs Biophysical Factors 生化 vs 生物物理因子

Aspect 方面Biochemical Factors 生化因子Biophysical Factors 生物物理因子
Definition 定義Chemical signals like growth factors 化學信號，如生長因子Physical stimuli like mechanical loading 物理刺激，如機械負荷
Role 作用Promote proliferation/differentiation 促進增殖/分化Influence shape, viability, and signaling 影響形狀、活力和信號傳導
Examples 例子VEGF, BMPShear stress 剪切應力, cyclic stretch 週期性拉伸
Mechanism 機制Bind receptors, activate pathways 結合受體，激活通路Transmit via cytoskeleton to nucleus 通過細胞骨架傳遞至細胞核
Exam Focus 考試重點Pathway details 通路細節Tensegrity integration 張拉整體整合

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 混淆生物物理因子同生化因子 (Mistaking biophysical for biochemical): 考官可能會要求區分兩者效果；唔好混淆機械負荷同生長因子。
  • 忽略整合性 (Overlooking integration): 陷阱題目會問範式而不提及所有四個組成部分。
  • 假設所有因子作用相同 (Assuming all factors are equal): 答案中經常會忽視生物物理因子嘅重要性。

Topic 2 – Tensegrity Model 張拉整體模型

大圖景 (Big Picture)

The tensegrity model 張拉整體模型 describes cells as prestressed structures 預應力結構 where tension 張力 and compression 壓力 elements balance forces, enabling rapid mechanical signal transmission for mechanotransduction 機械轉導.

思維導圖 (Mind map):
Tensegrity 張拉整體 → Architectural 結構性 (tension-compression synergy 張力-壓力協同) → Cellular 細胞性 (actin tension 肌動蛋白張力, microtubules compression 微管壓力) → Pre-stress 預應力 (contractile tension 收縮張力) → Force Propagation 力傳播 (rapid, long-distance 快速，長距離)

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • Championed by Don Ingber as alternative to elastic bag model 彈性袋模型.
  • Cells and nuclei are “hard-wired 硬連線“ to respond to physical stimuli via integrins 整合素.
  • Tension elements 張力元素: actin filaments 肌動蛋白絲; compression 壓力元素: microtubules 微管.
  • Pre-stress 預應力 from actomyosin contraction 肌動球蛋白收縮 balances forces.
  • Stabilizes cell structure and morphology 形態.
  • Forces transmitted at ECM/cell contacts ECM/細胞接觸點, not equally over surface.
  • Inhibited by integrin antagonists 整合素拮抗劑 (shear stress, strain effects).
  • Provides basis for mechanotransduction 機械轉導 linking physical to molecular events.
  • Explains transmission to nucleus.
  • Synergy between balanced tension and compression.
  • Architectural system 結構系統 stabilizes by counteracting forces.
  • Applies to natural and artificial forms (Buckminster Fuller).
  • Prestressed structures 預應力結構 promote long-distance force propagation 長距離力傳播.
  • Faster than chemical signals (1000x via cytoskeleton).
  • Contrasts with homogeneous material models 均質材料模型 where forces decay rapidly.

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - Tensegrity vs Elastic Bag Model 張拉整體 vs 彈性袋模型

Aspect 方面Tensegrity Model 張拉整體模型Elastic Bag Model 彈性袋模型
Structure 結構Prestressed tension-compression 預應力張力-壓力Viscous fluid in bag 袋中黏性流體
Force Transmission 力傳遞Rapid along cytoskeleton 沿細胞骨架快速傳遞Slow diffusion 緩慢擴散
Response 響應Immediate to physical stimuli 對物理刺激即時響應Delayed chemical 化學延遲
Key Elements 關鍵元素Actin, microtubules, integrins 肌動蛋白，微管，整合素Membrane, cytosol 膜，細胞質
Applications 應用Mechanotransduction explanation 解釋機械轉導Basic cell mechanics 基礎細胞力學

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 忘記預應力 (Forgetting pre-stress): 陷阱題在解釋力平衡時不提肌動球蛋白。
  • 混淆元素 (Confusing elements): 混淆張力（肌動蛋白）和壓力（微管）。
  • 低估速度 (Underestimating speed): 考官測試點解機械信號傳遞快過化學信號。

Topic 3 – Mechanoreceptors 機械感受器

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Mechanoreceptors 機械感受器 are plasma membrane proteins 質膜蛋白 that sense external mechanical signals and relay them intracellularly, including integrins 整合素, cadherins 鈣黏蛋白, and stretch-activated ion channels 牽張激活離子通道.

思維導圖 (Mind map):
Mechanoreceptors 機械感受器 → Integrins 整合素 (ECM-cytoskeleton link ECM-細胞骨架連結) → Cadherins 鈣黏蛋白 (cell-cell adhesion 細胞間黏附) → Ion Channels 離子通道 (ion flux on stretch 拉伸時離子流) → Outcomes 結果 (signaling 信號傳導, gene expression 基因表達)

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • Located in plasma membrane 質膜 to sense and relay signals.
  • Integrins 整合素: Link ECM to cytoskeleton via focal adhesions 黏著斑, alter cytoskeleton directly.
  • Cadherins 鈣黏蛋白: Form adherens junctions 黏附連接 for cell-cell force transmission.
  • Stretch-activated ion channels 牽張激活離子通道: Selective for Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cl-; involved in signaling and gene expression.
  • Activated by membrane deformation 膜變形; may activate or inactivate on stretch.
  • Produce intracellular consequences like Ca2+ flux 鈣離子流.
  • Regulate actin polymerization 肌動蛋白聚合 and contact stability 接觸穩定性.
  • Involved in cell signaling, transcription 轉錄, translation 翻譯, protein synthesis 蛋白質合成.
  • Electrochemical gradient 電化學梯度 drives ion movement.
  • Conformational changes 構象變化 with deformation.
  • High ion selectivity 離子選擇性.
  • Essential for mechanotransduction 機械轉導.
  • Cadherins may regulate proliferative signaling 增殖信號 via β-catenin β-連環蛋白.
  • Integrins transmit shear stress 剪切應力 signals.

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - Integrins vs Cadherins 整合素 vs 鈣黏蛋白

Aspect 方面Integrins 整合素Cadherins 鈣黏蛋白
Function 功能ECM-cytoskeleton link ECM-細胞骨架連結Cell-cell adhesion 細胞間黏附
Location 位置Focal adhesions 黏著斑Adherens junctions 黏附連接
Response 響應Alter cytoskeleton 改變細胞骨架Regulate force transmission 調節力傳遞
Signaling 信號傳導FAK, ERK activation FAK, ERK激活β-catenin to nucleus β-連環蛋白入核
Examples 例子Shear stress induction 誘導剪切應力響應Tension stabilization 張力穩定化

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 混淆整合素同鈣黏蛋白 (Confusing integrins with cadherins): 陷阱題問細胞間 vs 細胞-ECM。
  • 忽略離子選擇性 (Ignoring ion selectivity): 考官會問特定離子（Na+, K+, Ca2+）。
  • 忽略失活 (Overlooking deactivation): 部分通道拉伸時會失活。

Topic 4 – Integrins 整合素 and Focal Adhesions 黏著斑

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Integrins 整合素 are transmembrane proteins 跨膜蛋白 mediating cell-ECM binding 細胞-ECM結合 and force transmission, forming focal adhesions 黏著斑 that mature under tension 張力 to activate signaling pathways 信號通路.

思維導圖 (Mind map):
Integrins 整合素 → Ligand Binding 配體結合 → Focal Complexes 黏著複合體 (precursor 前體) → Focal Adhesions 黏著斑 (mature 成熟) → Signaling 信號 (FAK, ERK) → Cellular Responses 細胞響應 (shape 形狀, motility 運動性)

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • Integrins elicit signaling similar to shear stress in endothelial cells 內皮細胞.
  • Transmit forces from ECM to cytoskeleton 細胞骨架.
  • Convert physical stimuli to bioelectrical/biochemical signals 生物電/生化信號.
  • Focal complexes 黏著複合體: Small, punctate 點狀, at cell periphery 細胞邊緣; precursors to FAs.
  • Move centripetally 向心地, elongate into FAs.
  • Focal adhesions 黏著斑: Larger, proximal 近端; require tension 張力 for development.
  • Formation: Integrin occupation 整合素佔據, clustering 聚集, tensile force 張力 exertion.
  • Involve proteins like vinculin, talin, paxillin.
  • Shear stress 剪切應力 recruits FAK to leading edge 前沿.
  • Tyrosine phosphorylation 酪氨酸磷酸化 leads to ERK activation.
  • FAK mutant FAK突變體 blocks ERK response to stress.
  • Integrin-ECM dynamics 整合素-ECM動態 altered by shear.
  • Forces travel to FAs via ECM or cytoskeleton.
  • Essential for mechanobiology 機械生物學 responses.

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - Focal Complexes vs Focal Adhesions 黏著複合體 vs 黏著斑

Aspect 方面Focal Complexes (FCs) 黏著複合體Focal Adhesions (FAs) 黏著斑
Size/Location 大小/位置Small, peripheral 細小，周邊Larger, proximal 更大，近端
Formation Time 形成時間Minutes after contact 接觸後幾分鐘Evolves from FCs 從FCs演化而來
Role 作用Precursor, leading edge 前體，前沿Mature, tension-dependent 成熟，依賴張力
Proteins 蛋白質Initial integrins 初始整合素FAK, vinculin, talin
Movement 運動Centripetal 向心Stable anchor 穩定錨定

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 混淆FC同FA (Mixing FC and FA): 陷阱題關於時間線或位置。
  • 忘記張力作用 (Forgetting tension role): 答案缺少”張力”對於成熟嘅描述。
  • 忽略信號傳導 (Ignoring signaling): 考官測試FAK-ERK聯繫。

Topic 5 – Mechanosignaling Pathways 機械信號通路

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Mechanosignaling pathways 機械信號通路 convert mechanical stimuli at cell surfaces into intracellular responses like gene expression 基因表達, involving integrins 整合素, VEGF, Wnt, and nuclear transmission 核傳遞.

思維導圖 (Mind map):
Stimuli 刺激 (Shear/Stretch 剪切/拉伸) → Receptors 受體 (Integrins/Cadherins 整合素/鈣黏蛋白) → Pathways 通路 (FAK/ERK, VEGF, Wnt) → Nucleus 細胞核 (SUN proteins, chromatin 染色質) → Responses 響應 (Differentiation/Proliferation 分化/增殖)

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • Mechanobiology 機械生物學: Responses to shear/stretch at focal adhesions.
  • Pathways: FAK activation, ERK via tyrosine phosphorylation 酪氨酸磷酸化.
  • Shear causes FAK recruitment and rear disappearance.
  • VEGF: Activates Ras-Raf-MEK-ERK pathway, phosphorylates Ets-1 for angiogenic genes 血管生成基因.
  • Nuclear transmission 核傳遞: SUN proteins link to lamina 核纖層, chromatin 染色質 via MARs (Matrix Attachment Regions) 基質附着區.
  • Nuclear actin/myosin 核肌動蛋白/肌球蛋白 control gene positioning 基因定位 and activation.
  • Adherens junctions 黏附連接: Similar to FAs, dynamic link to actin.
  • Tension 張力 at junctions stabilizes AJs, regulates β-catenin signaling β-連環蛋白信號.
  • Wnt pathway: β-catenin dissociation to nucleus for gene expression.
  • Cadherins require force for separation.
  • Vinculin localizes to AJs, binds cytoskeletal proteins.
  • Mechanical signals to nucleus within milliseconds 毫秒.
  • Involved in proliferation, apoptosis 凋亡, differentiation.
  • Examples: Ca2+ flux 鈣離子流 at junctions strengthens contacts.

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - Integrin vs Cadherin Pathways 整合素 vs 鈣黏蛋白通路

Aspect 方面Integrin Pathways 整合素通路Cadherin Pathways 鈣黏蛋白通路
Activation 激活Shear/stretch at FAs FAs處的剪切/拉伸Tension at AJs AJs處的張力
Key Proteins 關鍵蛋白質FAK, ERKβ-catenin, vinculin
Outcome 結果ERK activation, gene expression ERK激活，基因表達Proliferation via Wnt 通過Wnt增殖
Location 位置Cell-ECM 細胞-ECMCell-cell 細胞-細胞
Debate 爭議Clear force transmission 明確的力傳遞Tension response under debate 張力響應存在爭議

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 通路混淆 (Pathway mix-up): 混淆VEGF和Wnt；考官測試序列。
  • 忘記核連結 (Forgetting nuclear link): 陷阱題在解釋毫秒級響應時忽略。
  • 忽略β-連環蛋白 (Overlooking β-catenin): 答案缺少核轉位。

Topic 6 – Effects of Mechanical Forces on Cells 機械力對細胞嘅影響

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Mechanical forces 機械力 influence cell shape 細胞形狀, viability 活力, motility 運動性, differentiation 分化, and proliferation 增殖, switching genetic programs and guiding tissue formation.

思維導圖 (Mind map):
Forces 力 → Cell Shape 細胞形狀 (spreading/deformation 鋪展/變形) → Viability 活力 (apoptosis/growth 凋亡/生長) → Motility 運動性 (lamellipodia 板狀偽足) → Differentiation 分化 (osteo/myogenic 成骨/成肌) → Proliferation 增殖 (stress accumulation 應力積累)

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • Cell shape changes from force balance 力平衡; spreading if ECM stiffer than cytoskeleton.
  • Nucleus deforms, switches apoptosis/differentiation programs 凋亡/分化程序.
  • Geometric control 幾何控制: Micropatterns 微圖案 determine apoptosis vs growth via focal adhesions.
  • Motility 運動性: Lamellipodia 板狀偽足 at stress concentrations.
  • Differentiation 分化: Acto-myosin contractility 肌動球蛋白收縮性 promotes osteogenesis 成骨.
  • Myotube 肌管: Striations 橫紋 on muscle-stiffness gels 肌肉硬度凝膠.
  • Stiffness 硬度 directs lineage 譜系: Soft for neuron 神經元, medium muscle 肌肉, hard bone 骨.
  • Cadherins: Force induces Ca2+ flux 鈣離子流, actin polymerization 肌動蛋白聚合.
  • Traction microscopy 牽引顯微鏡: Measures force during differentiation.
  • Proliferation 增殖: High at edges/sharp geometries; Rho inhibition Rho抑制 abolishes.
  • Myocardium construct 心肌構建體: Neonatal cells + collagen, cyclic stretch 週期性拉伸 for synchronization.
  • Morphologically similar to adult tissue after 6 days.
  • Tissue shape determines stress accumulation 應力積累 for growth.
  • Mechanical precedent 機械先導 in differentiation.

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - Shape-Dependent Viability vs Differentiation 形狀依賴活力 vs 分化

Aspect 方面Shape-Dependent Viability 形狀依賴活力Shape-Dependent Differentiation 形狀依賴分化
Control 控制Micropatterned ECM 微圖案化ECMGeometric features/contractility 幾何特徵/收縮性
Outcome 結果Apoptosis vs growth 凋亡 vs 生長Osteogenesis, myogenesis 成骨，成肌
Key Factor 關鍵因子Focal adhesions 黏著斑Acto-myosin tension 肌動球蛋白張力
Examples 例子Small patterns → apoptosis 小圖案→凋亡High contractility → bone 高收縮性→骨
Stiffness Role 硬度作用N/AMatches tissue (e.g., muscle gels) 匹配組織（如肌肉凝膠）

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 忽略硬度 (Ignoring stiffness): 陷阱題在解釋分化時不提kPa數值。
  • 泛化增殖 (Generalizing proliferation): 增殖在邊緣高，並非均勻。
  • 忘記先導性 (Forgetting precedence): 機械變化先於分化發生。

Topic 7 – Scaffolds Introduction and Requirements 支架簡介與要求

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Scaffolds 支架 are artificial ECMs 人工細胞外基質 providing 3D support for cell growth, nutrient diffusion 營養擴散, and tissue regeneration, with requirements for porosity 孔隙率, biocompatibility 生物相容性, and mechanical matching 機械匹配.

思維導圖 (Mind map):
Scaffolds 支架 → Functions 功能 (support 支撐, degrade 降解, diffuse 擴散) → Requirements 要求 (3D porous 3D多孔, biocompatible 生物相容性, surface chemistry 表面化學, mechanical 機械性) → Biomimetic 仿生 (mimic ECM 模仿ECM) → Applications 應用 (bone 骨, vascular 血管)

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • Temporary/permanent artificial ECM 人工ECM for 3D regeneration.
  • ECM 細胞外基質: Macromolecules (proteins/carbs 蛋白質/碳水化合物) as space fillers 空間填充物.
  • 2D/3D structures; promote cell growth, degrade.
  • Allow nutrient/waste diffusion 營養/廢物擴散, cell migration 細胞遷移.
  • Encourage blood vessel formation 血管形成.
  • Biomimetic 仿生: Apply natural methods to engineering.
  • Examples: Trabecular 小梁結構 for bone, triphasic 三相 for bone-ligament.
  • Requirements: 3D porous interconnected network 多孔互連網絡.
  • Biocompatible 生物相容性, bioresorbable 生物可吸收 with controllable degradation.
  • Suitable surface for attachment/proliferation/differentiation 黏附/增殖/分化.
  • Mechanical properties 機械特性 match implantation site 植入部位.
  • High porosity 孔隙率 for cell colonization 細胞定植.
  • Tunable biodegradability 生物降解性.
  • Biocompatibility 生物相容性 prevents immune reaction 免疫反應.

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - 2D vs 3D Scaffolds

Aspect 方面2D Scaffolds 2D支架3D Scaffolds 3D支架
Structure 結構Substrate-like 基底樣Volumetric 體積性
Function 功能Basic support 基礎支撐Full tissue regeneration 完全組織再生
Diffusion 擴散Limited 有限Enhanced via pores 通過孔隙增強
Applications 應用In vitro studies 體外研究In vivo implants 體內植入物
Challenges 挑戰No depth 無深度Vascularization 血管化

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 忘記互連性 (Forgetting interconnectivity): 僅有孔隙率不足；陷阱題關於擴散。
  • 機械特性不匹配 (Mismatching mechanics): 答案忽略部位特異性特性。
  • 忽略降解 (Overlooking degradation): 必須與組織形成同步。

Topic 8 – Scaffold Properties and Structures 支架特性與結構

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Scaffold properties 支架特性 include composition 組成 (polymers/ceramics/metals), macrostructure 宏觀結構, porosity/interconnectivity 孔隙率/互連性, and other factors like surface/volume ratio 表面積/體積比 and degradation 降解 for optimal tissue integration.

思維導圖 (Mind map):
Properties 特性 → Composition 組成 (natural/synthetic 天然/合成) → Structure 結構 (macro 宏觀, pores/tubes 孔/管) → Porosity 孔隙率 (200-500μm) → Others 其他 (ratio 比率, strength 強度, non-toxic degradation 無毒降解)

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • Composed of polymers 聚合物, ceramics 陶瓷, metals 金屬, composites 複合材料.
  • Match tissue: Polymers for soft, metals/ceramics for hard.
  • Porous 多孔 for cell entry, large surface area 表面積, interconnected 互連.
  • Composition: Pure non-organic, composites, organic; solid/gel.
  • Macrostructure 宏觀結構: External geometry congruent to tissue.
  • Porosity 孔隙率: Isolated/interconnected 孤立/互連; improves nutrition.
  • Pore sizes 孔徑 200-500μm for vascular ingrowth 血管長入.
  • Tubular structures 管狀結構 beneficial for bone.
  • High surface/volume ratio 表面積/體積比 for cell attachment.
  • Mechanical strength 機械強度 for load-bearing 承重 (cartilage/bone 軟骨/骨).
  • Degradation 降解 parallels tissue formation, retains integrity.
  • No toxic products 有毒產物 released.
  • Challenges: Control differentiation, porosity vs strength.
  • Processing: Consistent technologies.

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - Isolated vs Interconnected Pores 孤立孔 vs 互連孔

Aspect 方面Isolated Pores 孤立孔Interconnected Pores 互連孔
Nutrition 營養Limited diffusion 擴散有限Enhanced flow 流動增強
Cell Survival 細胞存活Poor in depths 深處差Better in regions 區域性好
Applications 應用Basic scaffolds 基礎支架Advanced regeneration 高級再生
Vascularization 血管化Low 低High (200-500μm) 高 (200-500μm)
Strength 強度Higher 較高May compromise 可能降低

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 孔徑錯誤 (Pore size error): 混淆血管長入孔徑 (200-500μm) 與一般孔徑。
  • 忽略比率 (Ignoring ratio): 陷阱題關於黏附而不提表面積/體積比。
  • 降解毒性 (Degradation toxicity): 忘記無毒要求。

Topic 9 – Types of Scaffolds 支架類型

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Scaffolds are classified by biocompatibility 生物相容性 as bioinert 生物惰性 (minimal interaction 最小相互作用), bioactive 生物活性 (interacts with tissue 與組織相互作用), or bioresorbable 生物可吸收 (dissolves and replaced by tissue 溶解並被組織取代), and by material as natural 天然 or synthetic 合成.

思維導圖 (Mind map):
Types 類型 → Biocompatibility 生物相容性 (Inert/Active/Resorbable 惰性/活性/可吸收) → Material 材料 (Natural/Synthetic 天然/合成) → Examples 例子 (Ti inert 鈦惰性, HA active 羥基磷灰石活性, TCP resorbable 磷酸三鈣可吸收)

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • Bioinert 生物惰性: Minimal tissue interaction (e.g., Titanium 鈦).
  • Bioactive 生物活性: Bonds to bone/soft tissue (e.g., HA 羥基磷灰石).
  • Bioresorbable 生物可吸收: Dissolves, replaced by tissue (e.g., Tricalcium phosphate (TCP) 磷酸三鈣).
  • Classification of bioceramics 生物陶瓷: Inert (dental implants 牙科植入物), bioactive (HA coating HA塗層), surface active 表面活性 (bioglass 生物玻璃), resorbable (TCP).
  • Natural 天然: Biologically active 生物活性, complex structure, high cost, pathogenic risks 致病風險.
  • Synthetic 合成: Modifiable, cheap, reproducible, but degradation/biocompatibility concerns 降解/生物相容性問題.
  • Natural examples: Collagen 膠原蛋白, agarose 瓊脂糖, heparin 肝素.
  • Synthetic: Metals, ceramics, polymers.
  • Natural suffice most criteria but limited modification.
  • Synthetic enable specific properties and shapes.
  • Bioinert: Fibrous capsule formation 纖維囊形成.
  • Bioactive: Bonding within 30 days.
  • Resorbable: Disappears 10-30 days.
  • Composites 複合材料 combine fillers in matrix.

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - Bioinert vs Bioactive vs Bioresorbable

Aspect 方面Bioinert 生物惰性Bioactive 生物活性Bioresorbable 生物可吸收
Interaction 相互作用Minimal 最小Bonds to tissue 與組織結合Dissolves/replaced 溶解/被取代
Examples 例子Titanium 鈦Hydroxyapatite (HA) 羥基磷灰石Tricalcium phosphate (TCP) 磷酸三鈣
Tissue Response 組織反應Fibrous capsule 纖維囊Bone ingrowth 骨長入Tissue advancement 組織前進
Duration 持續時間Permanent 永久Stable bond 穩定結合Temporary 暫時
Applications 應用Stents, instruments 支架，器械Coatings, restorations 塗層，修復體Bone grafts 骨移植物

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 錯誤分類 (Misclassifying): 混淆生物活性與生物可吸收；考官用例子測試。
  • 忽略風險 (Ignoring risks): 天然材料嘅致病性問題。
  • 誇大合成材料 (Overstating synthetic): 忘記毒性問題。

Topic 10 – Scaffold Materials (Metals and Ceramics) 支架材料（金屬與陶瓷）

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Scaffold materials 支架材料 include metals 金屬 (e.g., Ti 鈦, Mg 鎂) for strength and ceramics 陶瓷 (e.g., HA 羥基磷灰石, bioglass 生物玻璃) for bioactivity, each with advantages in biocompatibility but limitations in toxicity or mechanics.

思維導圖 (Mind map):
Materials 材料 → Metals 金屬 (Ti scaffolds 鈦支架, Mg alloys 鎂合金) → Ceramics 陶瓷 (CaP 磷酸鈣, HA, bioglass) → Properties 特性 (corrosion resistance 耐腐蝕性, resorbable 可吸收) → Applications 應用 (orthopedic 骨科, dental 牙科)

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • Metals 金屬: High strength, but lack biological recognition 生物識別; surface modification 表面改性 needed.
  • Possible toxic ion release 有毒離子釋放 from corrosion/wear 腐蝕/磨損.
  • Examples: 316L SS 316L不鏽鋼 for stents, Ti alloys for bone replacement.
  • Ti 鈦: High strength, light, corrosion resistant 耐腐蝕, biocompatible; porous for fixation.
  • Bioinert 生物惰性, slow bone bonding; BMP-loading BMP載藥 enhances.
  • Mg alloys 鎂合金: Bioresorbable 生物可吸收, osteoconductive 骨傳導性, but rapid dissolution risk (hypermagnesia 高鎂血症).
  • Ceramics 陶瓷: Refractory 耐火, inert, biocompatible; hard, brittle.
  • CaP 磷酸鈣: Bioactive 生物活性, resorbable; poor mechanics, used as coatings.
  • HA 羥基磷灰石: Similar to bone mineral, supports ingrowth; for augmentation 增補.
  • Bioglass 生物玻璃: Bonds to tissues, composition SiO2-CaO-Na2O; bone cement 骨水泥.
  • Bioactivity regions 生物活性區域: A (bonding 結合), B (inert 惰性), C (resorbable 可吸收), D (non-glass 非玻璃).
  • Resorbable 可吸收: Replaced by bone without mechanical loss.
  • Metals for hard tissues, polymers for soft.
  • Challenges: Toxicity, corrosion for metals; brittleness 脆性 for ceramics.

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - Metals vs Ceramics 金屬 vs 陶瓷

Aspect 方面Metals 金屬Ceramics 陶瓷
Properties 特性Strong, deformable 強，可變形Hard, brittle 硬，脆
Biocompatibility 生物相容性Bioinert, modifiable 生物惰性，可改性Bioactive/resorbable 生物活性/可吸收
Examples 例子Ti, MgHA, bioglass
Limitations 局限性Toxic ions, corrosion 有毒離子，腐蝕Poor load-bearing 承載能力差
Applications 應用Bone plates, stents 骨板，支架Coatings, bone cement 塗層，骨水泥

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 忘記改性 (Forgetting modifications): 金屬需要塗層以獲得生物活性。
  • 鎂毒性 (Mg toxicity): 陷阱題在提及吸收時不提及排泄/高鎂血症。
  • HA vs TCP (HA vs TCP): 混淆不可吸收嘅HA與可吸收嘅TCP。

Active Recall Bank 主動回憶庫

  • What are the four components of the tissue engineering paradigm? (Cells, Biocompatible Materials, Biochemical Factors, Biophysical Factors)
  • Who championed the tensegrity model? (Don Ingber)
  • Tension elements in tensegrity: (Actin filaments)
  • Compression elements: (Microtubules)
  • What is pre-stress in cells? (Contractile tension from actomyosin)
  • Three main mechanoreceptors: (Integrins, Cadherins, Stretch-activated ion channels)
  • Integrins link (ECM) to (cytoskeleton)
  • Focal complexes evolve into (Focal Adhesions)
  • Key pathway in mechanosignaling: FAK to (ERK)
  • VEGF activates (Ras-Raf-MEK-ERK) pathway
  • Cell shape changes switch programs for (apoptosis) or (growth/differentiation)
  • Stiffness for neuron differentiation: (Soft, ~0.1-1 kPa)
  • Scaffolds mimic (natural ECM)
  • Scaffold requirements: 3D (porous interconnected network), biocompatible, (mechanical match)
  • Ideal pore size for vascular ingrowth: (200-500 μm)
  • Bioinert example: (Titanium)
  • Bioactive: (Hydroxyapatite, HA)
  • Bioresorbable: (Tricalcium phosphate, TCP)
  • Natural materials risk: (Pathogenic contamination 病原體污染)
  • Synthetic advantage: (Modifiability, reproducibility 可改性，可重現性)

15-Minute Mock Exam 15分鐘模擬考試

  1. Explain the tensegrity model and its role in mechanotransduction.

    The tensegrity model views cells as prestressed structures with actin (tension) and microtubules (compression), enabling rapid force transmission from surface to nucleus, linking physical stimuli to molecular events.

  2. Describe focal adhesions and their formation.

    Focal adhesions are mature integrin-based structures requiring tension; formed by ligand occupation, clustering, and force exertion, involving proteins like FAK and vinculin for signaling.

  3. How do mechanical forces influence cell differentiation?

    Forces alter shape and contractility; e.g., high contractility promotes osteogenesis, while stiffness directs lineages (soft: neuron, medium: muscle, hard: bone) via mechanotransduction.

  4. What are the requirements for an ideal scaffold?

    3D porous interconnected network, biocompatible/bioresorbable with matched degradation, suitable surface chemistry, and mechanical properties aligning with implantation site.

  5. Compare natural and synthetic scaffold materials.

    Natural: Biologically active but costly and risky; synthetic: Modifiable, cheap, but potential toxicity from degradation.

Tensegrity 張拉整體Actin 肌動蛋白 (tension 張力) + Microtubules 微管 (compression 壓力) + Pre-stress 預應力 → Rapid force propagation 快速力傳播
Receptors 受體Integrins 整合素 (ECM), Cadherins 鈣黏蛋白 (cell-cell 細胞間), Ion channels 離子通道 (stretch-activated 牽張激活)
Signaling 信號通路FAK/ERK from FAs 黏著斑; VEGF: Ras-Raf-MEK-ERK; Wnt: β-catenin nuclear β-連環蛋白入核

Scaffolds 支架Porous 多孔 (200-500μm), biocompatible 生物相容性, degrade match growth 降解匹配生長, mechanical site-match 機械部位匹配

Materials 材料Natural 天然 (collagen 膠原蛋白, active 活性); Synthetic 合成 (modifiable 可改性); Metals 金屬 (Ti strong 鈦強, Mg resorb 鎂可吸收); Ceramics 陶瓷 (HA bone-like HA骨樣, brittle 脆)

Topic 1 – Tissue Engineering Paradigm

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

The tissue engineering paradigm integrates cells, biocompatible materials, biochemical factors like growth factors, and biophysical factors such as cyclic mechanical loading to regenerate tissues.

Mind map: Paradigm → Cells (source) → Materials (scaffolds) → Biochemical (growth factors) → Biophysical (mechanical loading) → Outcomes (tissue regeneration)

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • Core components: cells, biocompatible materials, biochemical factors (e.g., growth factors), biophysical factors (e.g., cyclic mechanical loading).
  • Aims to regenerate 3D tissues using a multidisciplinary approach.
  • Cells provide the building blocks for new tissue formation.
  • Materials act as scaffolds for cell attachment and growth.
  • Biochemical factors stimulate cell proliferation and differentiation.
  • Biophysical factors mimic in vivo mechanical environments to guide tissue development.
  • Paradigm emphasizes biomimicry of natural ECM.
  • Applications include hard tissues like bone and soft tissues like vascular.
  • Challenges: controlling differentiation, proliferation, and immune response.
  • Mechanical environment plays a key role in cell signaling and phenotype.
  • Scaffolds must support nutrient diffusion and vascularization.
  • Cyclic stretching is essential for constructs like myocardium.
  • Integration of factors ensures synchronized tissue function.
  • Paradigm supports both in vitro and in vivo regeneration.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

AspectBiochemical FactorsBiophysical Factors
DefinitionChemical signals like growth factorsPhysical stimuli like mechanical loading
RolePromote proliferation/differentiationInfluence shape, viability, and signaling
ExamplesVEGF, BMPShear stress, cyclic stretch
MechanismBind receptors, activate pathwaysTransmit via cytoskeleton to nucleus
Exam FocusPathway detailsTensegrity integration

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Mistaking biophysical for biochemical: Examiners may ask to differentiate effects; don’t confuse mechanical loading with growth factors.
  • Overlooking integration: Trap in questions on paradigm without mentioning all four components.
  • Assuming all factors are equal: Biophysical often underemphasized in answers.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • CB3: Cells, Biocompatible materials, Biochemical, Biophysical – “Can’t Build Without CB3”.

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

What are the key components of the tissue engineering paradigm?

The paradigm includes cells for tissue building, biocompatible materials as scaffolds, biochemical factors like growth factors for signaling, and biophysical factors such as mechanical loading to mimic in vivo conditions.

Explain the role of biophysical factors in tissue engineering.

Biophysical factors like cyclic mechanical loading guide cell behavior, promote differentiation (e.g., myotube formation), and ensure functional tissue constructs, as seen in engineered heart tissue requiring stretching for synchronization.

Topic 2 – Tensegrity Model

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

The tensegrity model describes cells as prestressed structures where tension and compression elements balance forces, enabling rapid mechanical signal transmission for mechanotransduction.

Mind map: Tensegrity → Architectural (tension-compression synergy) → Cellular (actin tension, microtubules compression) → Pre-stress (contractile tension) → Force Propagation (rapid, long-distance)

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • Championed by Don Ingber as alternative to elastic bag model.
  • Cells and nuclei are “hard-wired” to respond to physical stimuli via integrins.
  • Tension elements: actin filaments; compression: microtubules.
  • Pre-stress from actomyosin contraction balances forces.
  • Stabilizes cell structure and morphology.
  • Forces transmitted at ECM/cell contacts, not equally over surface.
  • Inhibited by integrin antagonists (shear stress, strain effects).
  • Provides basis for mechanotransduction linking physical to molecular events.
  • Explains transmission to nucleus.
  • Synergy between balanced tension and compression.
  • Architectural system stabilizes by counteracting forces.
  • Applies to natural and artificial forms (Buckminster Fuller).
  • Prestressed structures promote long-distance force propagation.
  • Faster than chemical signals (1000x via cytoskeleton).
  • Contrasts with homogeneous material models where forces decay rapidly.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

AspectTensegrity ModelElastic Bag Model
StructurePrestressed tension-compressionViscous fluid in bag
Force TransmissionRapid along cytoskeletonSlow diffusion
ResponseImmediate to physical stimuliDelayed chemical
Key ElementsActin, microtubules, integrinsMembrane, cytosol
ApplicationsMechanotransduction explanationBasic cell mechanics

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Forgetting pre-stress: Trap in explaining force balance without mentioning actomyosin.
  • Confusing elements: Mixing tension (actin) with compression (microtubules).
  • Underestimating speed: Examiners test on why mechanical > chemical propagation.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • TAM: Tension Actin, Microtubules compression – “Tense Actors Manage”.

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

Describe the tensegrity model in cells.

Cells maintain structure via tension (actin filaments) and compression (microtubules) elements, with pre-stress from actomyosin, allowing rapid force transmission through a hard-wired network.

How does pre-stress contribute to force propagation?

Pre-stress stiffens the cytoskeleton, enabling long-distance signal travel without rapid decay, unlike in non-prestressed models.

Topic 3 – Mechanoreceptors

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Mechanoreceptors are plasma membrane proteins that sense external mechanical signals and relay them intracellularly, including integrins, cadherins, and stretch-activated ion channels.

Mind map: Mechanoreceptors → Integrins (ECM-cytoskeleton link) → Cadherins (cell-cell adhesion) → Ion Channels (ion flux on stretch) → Outcomes (signaling, gene expression)

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • Located in plasma membrane to sense and relay signals.
  • Integrins: Link ECM to cytoskeleton via focal adhesions, alter cytoskeleton directly.
  • Cadherins: Form adherens junctions for cell-cell force transmission.
  • Stretch-activated ion channels: Selective for Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cl-; involved in signaling and gene expression.
  • Activated by membrane deformation; may activate or inactivate on stretch.
  • Produce intracellular consequences like Ca2+ flux.
  • Regulate actin polymerization and contact stability.
  • Involved in cell signaling, transcription, translation, protein synthesis.
  • Electrochemical gradient drives ion movement.
  • Conformational changes with deformation.
  • High ion selectivity.
  • Essential for mechanotransduction.
  • Cadherins may regulate proliferative signaling via β-catenin.
  • Integrins transmit shear stress signals.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

AspectIntegrinsCadherins
FunctionECM-cytoskeleton linkCell-cell adhesion
LocationFocal adhesionsAdherens junctions
ResponseAlter cytoskeletonRegulate force transmission
SignalingFAK, ERK activationβ-catenin to nucleus
ExamplesShear stress inductionTension stabilization

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Confusing integrins with cadherins: Trap in cell-cell vs cell-ECM questions.
  • Ignoring ion selectivity: Examiners ask for specific ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+).
  • Overlooking deactivation: Some channels inactivate on stretch.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • ICI: Integrins-Cell-ECM, Cadherins-Cell-Cell, Ion Channels-Ions – “I See Ions”.

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

What are the main types of mechanoreceptors?

Integrins for ECM linkage, cadherins for cell-cell adhesion, and stretch-activated ion channels for ion flux.

How do stretch-activated ion channels function?

They span the membrane, open on deformation allowing selective ion movement based on gradients, influencing signaling and gene expression.

Topic 4 – Integrins and Focal Adhesions

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Integrins are transmembrane proteins mediating cell-ECM binding and force transmission, forming focal adhesions that mature under tension to activate signaling pathways.

Mind map: Integrins → Ligand Binding → Focal Complexes (precursor) → Focal Adhesions (mature) → Signaling (FAK, ERK) → Cellular Responses (shape, motility)

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • Integrins elicit signaling similar to shear stress in endothelial cells.
  • Transmit forces from ECM to cytoskeleton.
  • Convert physical stimuli to bioelectrical/biochemical signals.
  • Focal complexes: Small, punctate, at cell periphery; precursors to FAs.
  • Move centripetally, elongate into FAs.
  • Focal adhesions: Larger, proximal; require tension for development.
  • Formation: Integrin occupation, clustering, tensile force exertion.
  • Involve proteins like vinculin, talin, paxillin.
  • Shear stress recruits FAK to leading edge.
  • Tyrosine phosphorylation leads to ERK activation.
  • FAK mutant blocks ERK response to stress.
  • Integrin-ECM dynamics altered by shear.
  • Forces travel to FAs via ECM or cytoskeleton.
  • Essential for mechanobiology responses.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

AspectFocal ComplexesFocal Adhesions
Size/LocationSmall, peripheralLarger, proximal
Formation TimeMinutes after contactEvolves from FCs
RolePrecursor, leading edgeMature, tension-dependent
ProteinsInitial integrinsFAK, vinculin, talin
MovementCentripetalStable anchor

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Mixing FC and FA: Trap in timeline or location questions.
  • Forgetting tension role: Answers missing “tensile forces” for maturation.
  • Ignoring signaling: Examiners test on FAK-ERK link.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • FC to FA: “Fast Cats (FCs) Become Fat Adults (FAs) Under Tension”.

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

Differentiate focal complexes from focal adhesions.

Focal complexes are small, peripheral precursors formed minutes after ECM contact, while focal adhesions are larger, mature structures requiring tension and integrin clustering.

How do integrins transmit mechanical signals?

Through focal adhesions, where forces activate FAK and ERK pathways, converting physical stimuli to biochemical signals.

Topic 5 – Mechanosignaling Pathways

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Mechanosignaling pathways convert mechanical stimuli at cell surfaces into intracellular responses like gene expression, involving integrins, VEGF, Wnt, and nuclear transmission.

Mind map: Stimuli (Shear/Stretch) → Receptors (Integrins/Cadherins) → Pathways (FAK/ERK, VEGF, Wnt) → Nucleus (SUN proteins, chromatin) → Responses (Differentiation/Proliferation)

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • Mechanobiology: Responses to shear/stretch at focal adhesions.
  • Pathways: FAK activation, ERK via tyrosine phosphorylation.
  • Shear causes FAK recruitment and rear disappearance.
  • VEGF: Activates Ras-Raf-MEK-ERK, phosphorylates Ets-1 for angiogenic genes.
  • Nuclear transmission: SUN proteins link to lamina, chromatin via MARs.
  • Nuclear actin/myosin control gene positioning and activation.
  • Adherens junctions: Similar to FAs, dynamic link to actin.
  • Tension at junctions stabilizes AJs, regulates β-catenin signaling.
  • Wnt pathway: β-catenin dissociation to nucleus for gene expression.
  • Cadherins require force for separation.
  • Vinculin localizes to AJs, binds cytoskeletal proteins.
  • Mechanical signals to nucleus within milliseconds.
  • Involved in proliferation, apoptosis, differentiation.
  • Examples: Ca2+ flux at junctions strengthens contacts.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

AspectIntegrin PathwaysCadherin Pathways
ActivationShear/stretch at FAsTension at AJs
Key ProteinsFAK, ERKβ-catenin, vinculin
OutcomeERK activation, gene expressionProliferation via Wnt
LocationCell-ECMCell-cell
DebateClear force transmissionTension response under debate

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Pathway mix-up: Confusing VEGF with Wnt; examiners test sequence.
  • Forgetting nuclear link: Trap in explaining millisecond response.
  • Overlooking β-catenin: Answers missing nuclear translocation.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • VRRME: VEGF-R2-Ras-Raf-MEK-ERK – “Very Rapid Response Makes Everything”.

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

Outline the VEGF kinase signaling pathway.

VEGF binds VEGF-R2, localizes Sos to activate Ras-GTP, which binds Raf to phosphorylate MEK, then ERK, leading to Ets-1 phosphorylation and angiogenic gene expression.

How do mechanical signals reach the nucleus?

Via cytoskeleton linked to SUN proteins, which connect to lamina and chromatin, enabling rapid gene activation through prestress regulation.

Topic 6 – Effects of Mechanical Forces on Cells

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Mechanical forces influence cell shape, viability, motility, differentiation, and proliferation, switching genetic programs and guiding tissue formation.

Mind map: Forces → Cell Shape (spreading/deformation) → Viability (apoptosis/growth) → Motility (lamellipodia) → Differentiation (osteo/myogenic) → Proliferation (stress accumulation)

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • Cell shape changes from force balance; spreading if ECM stiffer than cytoskeleton.
  • Nucleus deforms, switches apoptosis/differentiation programs.
  • Geometric control: Micropatterns determine apoptosis vs growth via focal adhesions.
  • Motility: Lamellipodia at stress concentrations.
  • Differentiation: Acto-myosin contractility promotes osteogenesis.
  • Myotube: Striations on muscle-stiffness gels.
  • Stiffness directs lineage: Soft for neuron, medium muscle, hard bone.
  • Cadherins: Force induces Ca2+ flux, actin polymerization.
  • Traction microscopy: Measures force during differentiation.
  • Proliferation: High at edges/sharp geometries; Rho inhibition abolishes.
  • Myocardium construct: Neonatal cells + collagen, cyclic stretch for synchronization.
  • Morphologically similar to adult tissue after 6 days.
  • Tissue shape determines stress accumulation for growth.
  • Mechanical precedent in differentiation.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

AspectShape-Dependent ViabilityShape-Dependent Differentiation
ControlMicropatterned ECMGeometric features/contractility
OutcomeApoptosis vs growthOsteogenesis, myogenesis
Key FactorFocal adhesionsActo-myosin tension
ExamplesSmall patterns → apoptosisHigh contractility → bone
Stiffness RoleN/AMatches tissue (e.g., muscle gels)

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Ignoring stiffness: Trap in differentiation without mentioning kPa values.
  • Generalizing proliferation: High at edges, not uniform.
  • Forgetting precedence: Mechanical changes before differentiation.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • SMDP: Shape-Motility-Differentiation-Proliferation – “Shapes Make Dolphins Play”.

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

How does cell shape affect viability?

On micropatterned substrates, restricted shape limits focal adhesions, promoting apoptosis, while spread shapes support growth.

Explain stiffness-directed differentiation.

Matrix stiffness mimics tissues: soft for neurons, medium for muscle, hard for bone, guiding stem cell lineage via mechanotransduction.

Topic 7 – Scaffolds Introduction and Requirements

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Scaffolds are artificial ECMs providing 3D support for cell growth, nutrient diffusion, and tissue regeneration, with requirements for porosity, biocompatibility, and mechanical matching.

Mind map: Scaffolds → Functions (support, degrade, diffuse) → Requirements (3D porous, biocompatible, surface chemistry, mechanical) → Biomimetic (mimic ECM) → Applications (bone, vascular)

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • Temporary/permanent artificial ECM for 3D regeneration.
  • ECM: Macromolecules (proteins/carbs) as space fillers.
  • 2D/3D structures; promote cell growth, degrade.
  • Allow nutrient/waste diffusion, cell migration.
  • Encourage blood vessel formation.
  • Biomimetic: Apply natural methods to engineering.
  • Examples: Trabecular for bone, triphasic for bone-ligament.
  • Requirements: 3D porous interconnected network.
  • Biocompatible, bioresorbable with controllable degradation.
  • Suitable surface for attachment/proliferation/differentiation.
  • Mechanical properties match implantation site.
  • High porosity for cell colonization.
  • Tunable biodegradability.
  • Biocompatibility prevents immune reaction.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

Aspect2D Scaffolds3D Scaffolds
StructureSubstrate-likeVolumetric
FunctionBasic supportFull tissue regeneration
DiffusionLimitedEnhanced via pores
ApplicationsIn vitro studiesIn vivo implants
ChallengesNo depthVascularization

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Forgetting interconnectivity: Porosity alone insufficient; traps in diffusion questions.
  • Mismatching mechanics: Answers ignoring site-specific properties.
  • Overlooking degradation: Must parallel tissue formation.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • PBMS: Porous, Biocompatible, Mechanical match, Surface chemistry – “Please Build My Scaffold”.

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

What are the key requirements for scaffolds?

3D porous interconnected for growth/nutrients, biocompatible/bioresorbable matching degradation to tissue growth, suitable surface chemistry, and mechanical properties matching implantation site.

Why is biomimicry important in scaffolds?

It mimics natural ECM to tailor cell responses, effectively engineering tissues like bone (trabecular) or vascular (hydrogels).

Topic 8 – Scaffold Properties and Structures

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Scaffold properties include composition (polymers/ceramics/metals), macrostructure, porosity/interconnectivity, and other factors like surface/volume ratio and degradation for optimal tissue integration.

Mind map: Properties → Composition (natural/synthetic) → Structure (macro, pores/tubes) → Porosity (200-500μm) → Others (ratio, strength, non-toxic degradation)

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • Composed of polymers, ceramics, metals, composites.
  • Match tissue: Polymers for soft, metals/ceramics for hard.
  • Porous for cell entry, large surface area, interconnected.
  • Composition: Pure non-organic, composites, organic; solid/gel.
  • Macrostructure: External geometry congruent to tissue.
  • Porosity: Isolated/interconnected; improves nutrition.
  • Pore sizes 200-500μm for vascular ingrowth.
  • Tubular structures beneficial for bone.
  • High surface/volume ratio for cell attachment.
  • Mechanical strength for load-bearing (cartilage/bone).
  • Degradation parallels tissue formation, retains integrity.
  • No toxic products released.
  • Challenges: Control differentiation, porosity vs strength.
  • Processing: Consistent technologies.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

AspectIsolated PoresInterconnected Pores
NutritionLimited diffusionEnhanced flow
Cell SurvivalPoor in depthsBetter in regions
ApplicationsBasic scaffoldsAdvanced regeneration
VascularizationLowHigh (200-500μm)
StrengthHigherMay compromise

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Pore size error: Confusing vascular (200-500μm) with general.
  • Ignoring ratio: Trap in attachment without surface/volume.
  • Degradation toxicity: Forgetting non-toxic requirement.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • PMPO: Porosity, Macrostructure, Properties, Other – “Please Make Pores Open”.

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

Describe scaffold porosity and interconnectivity.

Porosity allows cell growth and nutrient transport; interconnectivity ensures diffusion to deeper areas, with 200-500μm pores ideal for vascular ingrowth.

What other properties are crucial for scaffolds?

High surface/volume ratio for attachment, mechanical strength for load-bearing, tunable degradation without toxicity, matching tissue formation rate.

Topic 9 – Types of Scaffolds

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Scaffolds are classified by biocompatibility as bioinert (minimal interaction), bioactive (interacts with tissue), or bioresorbable (dissolves and replaced by tissue), and by material as natural or synthetic.

Mind map: Types → Biocompatibility (Inert/Active/Resorbable) → Material (Natural/Synthetic) → Examples (Ti inert, HA active, TCP resorbable)

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • Bioinert: Minimal tissue interaction (e.g., Titanium).
  • Bioactive: Bonds to bone/soft tissue (e.g., HA).
  • Bioresorbable: Dissolves, replaced by tissue (e.g., Tricalcium phosphate).
  • Classification of bioceramics: Inert (dental implants), bioactive (HA coating), surface active (bioglass), resorbable (TCP).
  • Natural: Biologically active, complex structure, high cost, pathogenic risks.
  • Synthetic: Modifiable, cheap, reproducible, but degradation/biocompatibility concerns.
  • Natural examples: Collagen, agarose, heparin.
  • Synthetic: Metals, ceramics, polymers.
  • Natural suffice most criteria but limited modification.
  • Synthetic enable specific properties and shapes.
  • Bioinert: Fibrous capsule formation.
  • Bioactive: Bonding within 30 days.
  • Resorbable: Disappears 10-30 days.
  • Composites combine fillers in matrix.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

AspectBioinertBioactiveBioresorbable
InteractionMinimalBonds to tissueDissolves/replaced
ExamplesTitaniumHydroxyapatiteTricalcium phosphate
Tissue ResponseFibrous capsuleBone ingrowthTissue advancement
DurationPermanentStable bondTemporary
ApplicationsStents, instrumentsCoatings, restorationsBone grafts

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Misclassifying: Confusing bioactive with bioresorbable; examiners use examples.
  • Ignoring risks: Natural materials’ pathogenic issues.
  • Overstating synthetic: Forgetting toxicity concerns.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • IAR: Inert-Active-Resorbable – “I Am Resorbable”.

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

Compare bioinert and bioresorbable scaffolds.

Bioinert have minimal interaction and remain permanent (e.g., Ti), while bioresorbable dissolve and are replaced by tissue (e.g., TCP).

Why choose natural over synthetic materials?

Natural offer biological activity and complex structures but at higher cost and risk; synthetic are modifiable and reproducible but may have degradation issues.

Topic 10 – Scaffold Materials (Metals and Ceramics)

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Scaffold materials include metals (e.g., Ti, Mg) for strength and ceramics (e.g., HA, bioglass) for bioactivity, each with advantages in biocompatibility but limitations in toxicity or mechanics.

Mind map: Materials → Metals (Ti scaffolds, Mg alloys) → Ceramics (CaP, HA, bioglass) → Properties (corrosion resistance, resorbable) → Applications (orthopedic, dental)

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • Metals: High strength, but lack biological recognition; surface modification needed.
  • Possible toxic ion release from corrosion/wear.
  • Examples: 316L SS for stents, Ti alloys for bone replacement.
  • Ti: High strength, light, corrosion resistant, biocompatible; porous for fixation.
  • Bioinert, slow bone bonding; BMP-loading enhances.
  • Mg alloys: Bioresorbable, osteoconductive, but rapid dissolution risk (hypermagnesia).
  • Ceramics: Refractory, inert, biocompatible; hard, brittle.
  • CaP: Bioactive, resorbable; poor mechanics, used as coatings.
  • HA: Similar to bone mineral, supports ingrowth; for augmentation.
  • Bioglass: Bonds to tissues, composition SiO2-CaO-Na2O; bone cement.
  • Bioactivity regions: A (bonding), B (inert), C (resorbable), D (non-glass).
  • Resorbable: Replaced by bone without mechanical loss.
  • Metals for hard tissues, polymers for soft.
  • Challenges: Toxicity, corrosion for metals; brittleness for ceramics.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

AspectMetalsCeramics
PropertiesStrong, deformableHard, brittle
BiocompatibilityBioinert, modifiableBioactive/resorbable
ExamplesTi, MgHA, bioglass
LimitationsToxic ions, corrosionPoor load-bearing
ApplicationsBone plates, stentsCoatings, bone cement

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Forgetting modifications: Metals need coatings for bioactivity.
  • Mg toxicity: Trap in resorption without mentioning excretion/hyper-magnesia.
  • HA vs TCP: Confusing non-resorbable HA with resorbable TCP.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • TMC: Ti-Mg-Ceramics – “Tough Metals Ceramics”.

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

Discuss titanium as a scaffold material.

Ti offers strength, lightness, and biocompatibility; porous structures allow ingrowth, but bioinert nature requires BMP or coatings for better bonding.

Compare metals and ceramics for scaffolds.

Metals provide strength but risk toxicity; ceramics are bioactive and resorbable but brittle, suited for coatings rather than load-bearing.

Active Recall Bank

  • What are the four components of the tissue engineering paradigm? _____
  • Who championed the tensegrity model? _____
  • Tension elements in tensegrity: _____
  • Compression elements: _____
  • What is pre-stress in cells? _____
  • Three main mechanoreceptors: _____, _____, _____
  • Integrins link _____ to _____
  • Focal complexes evolve into _____
  • Key pathway in mechanosignaling: FAK to _____
  • VEGF activates _____ pathway
  • Cell shape changes switch programs for _____ or _____
  • Stiffness for neuron differentiation: _____
  • Scaffolds mimic _____
  • Scaffold requirements: 3D _____, biocompatible, _____
  • Ideal pore size for vascular ingrowth: _____
  • Bioinert example: _____
  • Bioactive: _____
  • Bioresorbable: _____
  • Natural materials risk: _____
  • Synthetic advantage: _____

15-Minute Mock Exam

  1. Explain the tensegrity model and its role in mechanotransduction.

    The tensegrity model views cells as prestressed structures with actin (tension) and microtubules (compression), enabling rapid force transmission from surface to nucleus, linking physical stimuli to molecular events.

  2. Describe focal adhesions and their formation.

    Focal adhesions are mature integrin-based structures requiring tension; formed by ligand occupation, clustering, and force exertion, involving proteins like FAK and vinculin for signaling.

  3. How do mechanical forces influence cell differentiation?

    Forces alter shape and contractility; e.g., high contractility promotes osteogenesis, while stiffness directs lineages (soft: neuron, medium: muscle, hard: bone) via mechanotransduction.

  4. What are the requirements for an ideal scaffold?

    3D porous interconnected network, biocompatible/bioresorbable with matched degradation, suitable surface chemistry, and mechanical properties aligning with implantation site.

  5. Compare natural and synthetic scaffold materials.

    Natural: Biologically active but costly and risky; synthetic: Modifiable, cheap, but potential toxicity from degradation.

One-Page Cheat Sheet Summary

SectionKey Points
ParadigmCells + Materials + Biochemical (GFs) + Biophysical (loading) → Regeneration
TensegrityActin (tension) + Microtubules (compression) + Pre-stress → Rapid force propagation
ReceptorsIntegrins (ECM), Cadherins (cell-cell), Ion channels (stretch-activated)
SignalingFAK/ERK from FAs; VEGF: Ras-Raf-MEK-ERK; Wnt: β-catenin nuclear
Cell EffectsShape → Viability/Diff (soft neuron, hard bone); Proliferation at edges
ScaffoldsPorous (200-500μm), biocompatible, degrade match growth, mechanical site-match
TypesBioinert (Ti), Bioactive (HA), Bioresorbable (TCP)
MaterialsNatural (collagen, active); Synthetic (modifiable); Metals (Ti strong, Mg resorb); Ceramics (HA bone-like, brittle)
entry.summary: <h1 id="BME-2104-Tissue-Engineering-–-5"><a href="#BME-2104-Tissue-Engineering-–-5" class="headerlink" title="BME 2104 Tissue Engineering –

entry.title: BME 2104 Tissue Engineering – 6
entry.content:
BME 2104 Tissue Engineering – 6

Lecture Overview

  • 🔴 Polymer basics and definitions 聚合物基礎與定義
  • 🟢 Polymerization mechanisms 聚合機制
  • 🔵 Polymer structures and states in solids 聚合物固體結構與狀態
  • 🟢 Thermal and crystallinity properties 熱性能與結晶性
  • 🔵 Crosslinked networks and hydrogels 交聯網絡與水凝膠
  • 🔴 Natural polymer scaffolds and examples 天然聚合物支架與例子
  • 🟢 Synthetic polymer scaffolds and degradation 合成聚合物支架與降解
  • 🔵 3D cell cultures vs 2D and applications 3D vs 2D 細胞培養與應用

Topic 1 – Introduction to Polymers 聚合物簡介

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Polymers 聚合物 are large molecules essential for tissue engineering scaffolds due to their versatility in mimicking ECM 細胞外基質.

思維導圖 (Mind map):
Polymers 聚合物 → Natural 天然 (Wood/Rubber/DNA) → Synthetic 合成 (PE/PS/PEG) → Biomedical Suitability 生物醫學適用性 (Strength/Biodegradability/Fabrication 強度/生物降解性/加工性) → Disadvantages 缺點 (Deformation/Toxicity/Degradation 變形/毒性/降解)

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • Polymers revolutionized chemistry with major everyday impact (Lord Todd quote).
  • ECM 細胞外基質 components like collagen 膠原蛋白, proteins 蛋白質, and GAGs 糖胺聚糖 are polymers.
  • Suited for biomedical apps 生物醫學應用: low/high strength, protein attachment 蛋白質黏附 resistance/encouragement.
  • Biodegradable 生物可降解 or permanent; fabricable into complex shapes.
  • Disadvantages: lower strength than metals/ceramics, deform over time.
  • May deteriorate during sterilization 滅菌 or degrade catastrophically in body.
  • Release toxic byproducts 有毒副產物 possible.
  • Natural examples: Wood 木材, Rubber 橡膠, Leather 皮革, Cotton 棉花, Wool 羊毛, Silk 絲綢, DNA, Proteins 蛋白質.
  • Synthetic examples: Polystyrene (PS) 聚苯乙烯, PET 聚對苯二甲酸乙二醇酯, PE 聚乙烯, PEG 聚乙二醇, Kevlar 凱夫拉.
  • Polymers in scaffolds mimic ECM for cell support.

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - Natural vs Synthetic Polymers 天然 vs 合成聚合物

Natural Polymers 天然聚合物Synthetic Polymers 合成聚合物
From nature (e.g., wood, DNA) 來源於自然界 (如木材，DNA)Man-made (e.g., PE, PS) 人造 (如PE，PS)
Biocompatible but variable 生物相容性好但性質多變Consistent but may inflame 性質穩定但可能引起炎症
Often biodegradable 通常可生物降解Tunable degradation 可調節降解性

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 混淆聚合物與單體 (Confusing polymers with monomers): 陷阱題要求舉例聚合物時不指定類型。
  • 忽略缺點 (Overlooking disadvantages): 如體內產生有毒副產物。

Topic 2 – Polymerization Types 聚合類型

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Polymerization 聚合作用 forms chains via addition 加成聚合 (non-degradable) or step-growth 逐步聚合 (degradable via hydrolysis 水解).

思維導圖 (Mind map):
Polymerization 聚合作用 → Addition 加成聚合 (Radicals/Ions 自由基/離子 → No labile bonds 無不穩定鍵) → Step-Growth 逐步聚合 (Condensation 縮合 → Ester bonds 酯鍵 → Hydrolysis 水解) → Examples 例子 (PE vs PLA/PGA/PLGA)

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • Addition 加成聚合: Double-bond monomers 雙鍵單體 like ethylene 乙烯, vinyl chloride 氯乙烯, styrene 苯乙烯, MMA 甲基丙烯酸甲酯.
  • Via radicals 自由基 or ions 離子; no labile bonds, not degradable.
  • Example: Ethylene → Polyethylene (PE) 聚乙烯.
  • Step-growth 逐步聚合: Functional group reactions 官能團反應, condensation 縮合.
  • Well-known: PLA 聚乳酸, PGA 聚乙醇酸, PLGA 聚乳酸-乙醇酸共聚物.
  • Degrade via main chain ester hydrolysis 酯鍵水解.
  • PLA from lactic acid 乳酸 condensation.
  • PGA from glycolic acid 乙醇酸.
  • PLGA copolymers tunable.

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - Addition vs Step-Growth Polymerization 加成 vs 逐步聚合

Addition Polymerization 加成聚合Step-Growth Polymerization 逐步聚合
Double bonds break 雙鍵斷裂Functional groups react 官能團反應
Non-degradable chains 鏈不可降解Degradable via hydrolysis 可通過水解降解
Examples: PE, PVC 例子: PE，PVCExamples: PLA, PGA 例子: PLA，PGA

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 混淆加成聚合與逐步聚合 (Mixing addition and step-growth): 陷阱題將 PLA 分類為加成聚合（實際係逐步聚合）。
  • 忘記逐步聚合嘅副產物 (Forgetting condensation byproduct in step-growth): 有水生成。

Topic 3 – Polymer Structures and States 聚合物結構與狀態

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Polymers exist as crystalline 結晶, amorphous 無定形, or semi-crystalline 半結晶, affecting melting 熔化 and glass transitions 玻璃化轉變.

思維導圖 (Mind map):
Structures 結構 → Crystals 晶體 (Ordered/Lattice/Sharp Tm 有序/晶格/銳利Tm) → Amorphous 無定形 (No order/Tg 無序/Tg) → Polymers 聚合物 (Semi-crystalline/Folding/Stacking 半結晶/摺疊/堆疊) → States 狀態 (Rubber/Glass 橡膠態/玻璃態)

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • Crystals 晶體: Infinite 3D lattice 無限三維晶格, sharp Tm 熔點.
  • Amorphous 無定形: No structure, sharp Tm.
  • Small molecules 小分子: 100% crystalline or amorphous.
  • Polymer crystalline: Intramolecular folding/stacking 分子內摺疊/堆疊.
  • Semi-crystalline 半結晶: Never 100% crystalline, have Tm.
  • Rubber/Glass 橡膠態/玻璃態: Frozen liquid 凍結液體, no long-range order.
  • Tg 玻璃化轉變溫度: Below Tg, no cooperative rotation/translation 協同旋轉/平移.
  • Transition: Rubbery to glassy hard state.
  • Crystallization 結晶 favored by simple structures, small side groups.
  • Linear, non-crosslinked polymers crystallize better.
  • Block/alternating copolymers 嵌段/交替共聚物, slow cooling aid crystallization.
  • Crystalline: Stronger, resistant to dissolution.

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - Crystalline vs Amorphous 結晶 vs 無定形

Crystalline 結晶Amorphous 無定形
Ordered lattice 有序晶格No order 無序
Sharp Tm 銳利熔點Tg only 只有玻璃化轉變溫度
Stronger 更強Flexible 更柔韌

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 假設聚合物100%結晶 (Assuming polymers are 100% crystalline): 陷阱題 - 聚合物是半結晶的。
  • 混淆Tm同Tg (Confusing Tm and Tg)。

Topic 4 – Polymer Properties 聚合物特性

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Properties like crystallinity 結晶度, thermal behavior 熱行為, and side groups 側基 influence polymer performance in scaffolds.

思維導圖 (Mind map):
Properties 特性 → Crystallinity 結晶度 (Favored by simplicity 有利於簡單結構) → Side Groups 側基 (Flexible lower Tg 柔韌側基降低Tg) → Thermal 熱性能 (Tg/Tm values) → Effects 影響 (Free volume/Motion 自由體積/運動)

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • Crystallization 結晶: Simple/small side groups, linear, non-network.
  • Slow cooling for chain motion.
  • Crystalline stronger/resistant.
  • Flexible side groups: Tg decreases with length.
  • Side chains rotate, increase free volume 自由體積, lower Tg.
  • Thermal examples: Nylon 6,6 尼龍6,6 Tg 45°C Tm 267°C.
  • UHMWPE 超高分子量聚乙烯 Tg -125°C Tm 140°C.
  • Silicone 矽橡膠 Tg -123°C Tm -29°C.
  • PMMA 聚甲基丙烯酸甲酯 Tg 105°C Tm 160°C.
  • PDLLA 聚消旋乳酸 Tg 50°C amorphous.
  • PCL 聚己內酯 Tg -60°C Tm 57°C.
  • PGA 聚乙醇酸 Tg 35°C Tm 210°C.

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - Short vs Long Side Groups 短 vs 長側基

Short Side Groups 短側基Long Side Groups 長側基
Higher Tg 較高TgLower Tg 較低Tg
Less free volume 較少自由體積More free volume 較多自由體積
Restricted motion 運動受限Easier rotation 易於旋轉

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 認為長側基會提高Tg (Thinking longer sides increase Tg): 實際上會降低。
  • 忽略側基對堆積嘅影響 (Ignoring side chain effects on packing)。

Topic 5 – Crosslinked Networks and Hydrogels 交聯網絡與水凝膠

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Crosslinks 交聯鍵 create networks that swell in water to form hydrogels 水凝膠 for cell environments.

思維導圖 (Mind map):
Crosslinks 交聯鍵 (Covalent/H-bond/Entangle 共價鍵/氫鍵/纏結) → Networks 網絡 (Increased MW/Swell 分子量增加/溶脹) → Hydrogels 水凝膠 (Water-swollen/Balanced forces 水溶脹/平衡力) → Apps 應用 (Nutrients/Waste/Aqueous mimic 營養/廢物/水環境模擬)

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • Crosslinks: Covalent 共價鍵, H-bonding 氫鍵, entanglements 纏結.
  • Increase MW 分子量, swell in solvents (hydrogel).
  • Hydrogels 水凝膠: Water-swollen crosslinked structures.
  • From monomers or chain associations.
  • Why: Cells need aqueous for nutrients/waste.
  • Prepared by crosslinking then swelling.
  • PHEMA 聚羥基乙基甲基丙烯酸酯 common: Tissue-like water, degradation resistant.
  • Apps: Contacts 隱形眼鏡, wounds 傷口, kidney membranes 腎膜.
  • Swelling: Thermodynamic force 熱力學力 vs retractive 收縮力.
  • Highly swollen: Cellulose 纖維素, PVA 聚乙烯醇, PEG.
  • Moderately: PHEMA.

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - Solid Porous Scaffolds vs Hydrogel Scaffolds 固體多孔支架 vs 水凝膠支架

Solid Porous Scaffolds 固體多孔支架Hydrogel Scaffolds 水凝膠支架
For bone (stable) 用於骨（穩定）For soft tissues (fill defects) 用於軟組織（填充缺損）
Degrade by hydrolysis 通過水解降解Incorporate cells/bioactives easily 易於包埋細胞/生物活性因子
Non-dissolving 不溶解Minimally invasive 微創

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 混淆交聯與聚合 (Confusing crosslinks with polymerization): 陷阱題 - 水凝膠不需要交聯（實際需要）。
  • 忽略溶脹平衡 (Overlooking swelling balance)。

Topic 6 – Natural Polymer Scaffolds 天然聚合物支架

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Natural polymers from proteins 蛋白質/polysaccharides 多糖 provide biocompatible scaffolds but with variability risks.

思維導圖 (Mind map):
Natural Scaffolds 天然支架 → Proteins 蛋白質 (Collagen/Elastin 膠原蛋白/彈性蛋白) → Polysaccharides 多糖 (GAGs/Hyaluronan 糖胺聚糖/透明質酸) → Sources 來源 (Plants/Algae/Animals/Microbes 植物/藻類/動物/微生物) → Crosslinking 交聯 (Chemical/Physical 化學/物理)

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • Proteins: Amino acid polymers, include collagen, elastin, laminin.
  • DNA/RNA: Phosphate-ribose backbone, not for scaffolds.
  • Scaffolds: Proteins, polysaccharides, combos.
  • Sources: Collagen/fibrin/elastin (animals), soybean (plants), silk fibroin 絲素蛋白.
  • Gelatin 明膠: Partial hydrolysis of collagen from skin/bones.
  • Extraction: Reduce crosslinks, acid/water at temp.
  • Acidic pH speeds conversion.
  • Scaffolds: Crosslink via -OH/-NH2/-COOH/-SH.
  • Chemical mod tricky in water.
  • May alter solubility/conformation/activity 溶解性/構象/活性.
  • Collagen: Triple-helical 三螺旋, ECM main, immunogenicity risk 免疫原性風險.
  • Crosslink: Physical (moisture/heat/UV), chemical (glutaraldehyde 戊二醛).
  • Formaldehyde 甲醛: Fixes tissue by crosslinking aminos.
  • Polysaccharides: Monosaccharide polymers, glycosidic bonds 糖苷鍵.
  • GAGs: Unbranched 無分支, repeating disaccharides 重複雙糖單位 (chondroitin 硫酸軟骨素 etc.).

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - Proteins vs Polysaccharides 蛋白質 vs 多糖

Proteins 蛋白質Polysaccharides 多糖
Amino acids 氨基酸Monosaccharides 單糖
Collagen/Gelatin 膠原蛋白/明膠GAGs/Hyaluronan 糖胺聚糖/透明質酸
Crosslink via groups 通過官能團交聯Glycosidic bonds 糖苷鍵

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 認為DNA/RNA用作支架 (Thinking DNA/RNA used as scaffolds): 佢哋唔係。
  • 忽略天然聚合物嘅免疫原性 (Ignoring immunogenicity in natural polymers)。

Topic 7 – Specific Natural Polymers 特定天然聚合物

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Specific naturals like hyaluronan 透明質酸, matrigel 基質膠, fibrin 纖維蛋白, alginate 藻酸鹽, chitosan 殼聚糖 offer unique TE properties.

思維導圖 (Mind map):
Specifics 特定聚合物 → Hyaluronan 透明質酸 (Repair/Cartilage/Hydrophilic 修復/軟骨/親水) → Matrigel 基質膠 (ECM mix/Stem cells ECM混合物/幹細胞) → Fibrin 纖維蛋白 (Coagulation/Biocompatible 凝血/生物相容) → Alginate 藻酸鹽 (Algae/Ca crosslink 藻類/鈣交聯) → Chitosan 殼聚糖 (Arthropod/Cartilage 節肢動物/軟骨)

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • Hyaluronan 透明質酸: Polysaccharide in repair/ECM, needs peptides for attachment.
  • Crosslink: Acrylated HA 丙烯酸化HA with ADH/EDC, then thiol-acrylate 硫醇-丙烯酸酯.
  • RGD addition aids proliferation/migration.
  • Matrigel 基質膠: EHS sarcoma secretion, laminin/collagen/GFs 層粘連蛋白/膠原蛋白/生長因子.
  • For ESCs 胚胎幹細胞: Diluted maintains pluripotency 多能性, undiluted differentiates.
  • Fibrin 纖維蛋白: From fibrinogen/thrombin 纖維蛋白原/凝血酶, aggregates to coagulate.
  • Biocompatible, used as sealant 密封劑, but degrades by fibrinolysis 纖維蛋白溶解.
  • Alginate 藻酸鹽: From algae, hydrophilic, G-blocks crosslink with Ca (egg-carton 蛋盒模型).
  • M-blocks not involved.
  • Chitosan 殼聚糖: From exoskeletons 外骨骼, like GAGs, for cartilage/bone.

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - Fibrin vs Alginate 纖維蛋白 vs 藻酸鹽

Fibrin 纖維蛋白Alginate 藻酸鹽
From blood 來源於血液From algae 來源於藻類
Enzymatic cleavage 酶促裂解Ca crosslinking 鈣交聯
Degrades fast 降解快Stable 穩定

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 忘記透明質酸需要修飾才能黏附細胞 (Forgetting hyaluronan needs modification for attachment).
  • 混淆藻酸鹽嘅G區同M區 (Mixing G/M blocks in alginate)。

Topic 8 – Synthetic Polymer Scaffolds 合成聚合物支架

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Synthetics offer consistency over naturals, with tunable degradation 可調節降解 for TE.

思維導圖 (Mind map):
Synthetics 合成聚合物 → Less issues 問題較少 (No allergens/Batch var 無過敏原/批次差異) → Examples 例子 (PLA/PGA/PLGA/PCL/PEOT/PBT) → Degradation 降解 (Bulk/Surface 整體/表面) → Apps 應用 (Injectable/Long-term 可注射/長期)

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • Less byproducts, variations, immunogenicity than naturals.
  • PLA 聚乳酸: Semicrystalline, brittle 脆, hydrophobic 疏水, degrades to lactic acid 乳酸.
  • Copolymerize with PEG for solubility.
  • PGA 聚乙醇酸: Crystalline, tough 堅韌, degrades to glycolic acid 乙醇酸, for sutures 縫合線.
  • PLGA 聚乳酸-乙醇酸共聚物: Copolymers, biocompatible, acidic products inflame.
  • PCL 聚己內酯: Biocompatible, slow bulk erosion 整體侵蝕 (>3 yrs).
  • PEOT/PBT: Elastic, reversible crosslinks, tunable properties.
  • PPEs/PPAs 聚磷酸酯/聚磷酸酯膠: Surface erosion 表面侵蝕, milder response.
  • PPF-DA/PEG-DA 聚富馬酸丙二醇酯-二丙烯酸酯/聚乙二醇-二丙烯酸酯: Injectable, fill defects, low strength.
  • Categories: Porous solid (bone) vs hydrogels (soft tissues).
  • Solid: Stable, non-dissolve, hydrolyze.
  • Hydrogels: Fill defects, minimally invasive, incorporate cells.

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - Natural vs Synthetic Polymers (Scaffolds) 天然 vs 合成聚合物 (支架)

Natural 天然Synthetic 合成
Variable/biocompatible 多變/生物相容Consistent/tunable 穩定/可調節
Immunogenic risk 免疫原性風險Less inflammation 炎症較少
From sources 來源於天然Lab-made 實驗室製備

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 認為合成聚合物不可降解 (Thinking synthetics non-biodegradable): 許多是可降解的。
  • 忘記酸性降解產物 (Forgetting acidic degradation products): 可能引起炎症。

Topic 9 – 3D Cell Cultures and Applications 3D 細胞培養與應用

大圖景 (Big Picture)

3D cultures 3D培養 better mimic in vivo 體內 via scaffolds, improving cell behavior over 2D 二維.

思維導圖 (Mind map):
3D vs 2D → Why 原因 (ECM signals/Topography ECM信號/拓撲結構) → Limitations 局限性 (Diffusion 擴散) → Apps 應用 (Regen med/Drug discovery/Bioreactors 再生醫學/藥物發現/生物反應器)

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • 2D artificial 人造; 3D natural with ECM cues.
  • Control morphology/architecture/components.
  • Cells respond in vivo-like.
  • Matrices: Porous, support growth/differentiation.
  • Chemical mod: Adhesion/degradation/GF release.
  • Limited diffusion 擴散 in 3D; viability drops over time.
  • Apps: Clinical 臨床, in vitro 體外, regen med 再生醫學, drug discovery 藥物發現.
  • Bioreactors 生物反應器: Stirring/perfusion 攪拌/灌注 for mass transport 物質傳輸.
  • Focal adhesions 黏著斑 differ 2D vs 3D.
  • 3D for blood vessels/skin/cartilage 血管/皮膚/軟骨.

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - 2D vs 3D Cultures

2D Cultures 2D培養3D Cultures 3D培養
Flat/rigid 平坦/剛性Dynamic ECM 動態ECM
Misrepresent 不能準確代表In vivo-like 更接近體內
Simple 簡單Complex scaffolds 複雜支架

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 假設2D培養足夠 (Assuming 2D sufficient): 陷阱題 - 為何3D更好。
  • 忘記大型組織需要生物反應器 (Forgetting bioreactor need for large tissues)。

Active Recall Bank 主動回憶庫

  • What is a polymer? (Large molecule made of repeating monomer units)
  • Name two polymerization types: (Addition) and (Step-growth)
  • Tg stands for: (Glass transition temperature)
  • Factor favoring crystallinity: (Simple) structures
  • Hydrogels are (Water)-swollen networks
  • Natural polymer example: (Collagen)
  • Gelatin from: (Partial) hydrolysis of collagen
  • Hyaluronan needs (Peptides) for cell attachment
  • PLA degrades to: (Lactic) acid
  • PGA used for: (Sutures)
  • Synthetic advantage over natural: No (batch) variations
  • Crosslinks types: Covalent, (H-bonding), entanglements
  • Matrigel for: (Embryonic) stem cells
  • Fibrin from: (Fibrinogen) + thrombin
  • Alginate crosslinks with: (Calcium) ions
  • Chitosan from: (Arthropod) exoskeletons
  • 3D cultures mimic: (In vivo) environments
  • Limitation in 3D: Limited (diffusion)
  • Bioreactors provide: (Mass) transport
  • Focal adhesions differ in: (2D) vs (3D)

15-Minute Mock Exam 15分鐘模擬考試

  1. Describe two common techniques for fabricating polymeric scaffolds in tissue engineering.

    Examples include electrospinning 靜電紡絲 and solvent casting/particle leaching 溶劑鑄造/粒子浸出; these may not suit ceramics due to material differences.

  2. Compare advantages and limitations of natural vs synthetic polymers for scaffolds.

    Natural: Biocompatible, mimic ECM but variable/immunogenic; Synthetic: Consistent, tunable degradation but may cause inflammation from byproducts.

  3. What properties make an ideal scaffold for tissue engineering?

    Porous 多孔, biocompatible 生物相容, biodegradable at controlled rate 可控速率降解, mechanically suitable 機械性能合適, supports cell attachment/growth 支持細胞黏附/生長.

  4. Explain the role of hydrogels in scaffold-based tissue engineering.

    Hydrogels provide hydrated 3D environments mimicking soft tissues, allowing cell encapsulation 細胞封裝 and nutrient diffusion.

  5. Which polymer is commonly used for absorbable sutures and why?

    PGA; crystalline, tough, degrades to natural metabolite glycolic acid via hydrolysis.

  6. How does crystallinity affect polymer properties in scaffolds?

    Increases strength and resistance to dissolution 耐溶解; favored by simple linear structures and slow cooling.

  7. Describe the degradation mechanism for PLGA copolymers.

    Hydrolysis of ester bonds 酯鍵 in the main chain, releasing acidic products that may induce inflammation.

  8. Why are bioreactors used in 3D tissue engineering?

    To provide convective mass transport 對流物質傳輸 (nutrients/oxygen) via stirring/perfusion for larger-scale tissue development.

One-Page Cheat Sheet Summary 一頁速查表總結

Category 類別Key Points 要點 (中英對照)
Polymers Basics 聚合物基礎Natural 天然 (DNA/Collagen), Synthetic 合成 (PLA/PE); Suited for scaffolds: Versatile, but deform/toxic. 適用於支架：多功能，但會變形/有毒。
Polymerization 聚合Addition 加成 (PE, non-degradable), Step-growth 逐步 (PLA/PGA, hydrolyze esters).
Structures/States 結構/狀態Crystalline 結晶 (Tm), Amorphous 無定形 (Tg); Semi-crystalline 半結晶 polymers; Tg decreases with long sides.
Properties 特性Crystallinity 結晶度: Simple/linear favors; Thermal 熱性能: e.g., PGA Tg 35°C Tm 210°C.
Networks/Hydrogels 網絡/水凝膠Crosslinks swell; PHEMA common; Balance swelling/retractive forces. 交聯溶脹；PHEMA常用；平衡溶脹/收縮力。
Natural Scaffolds 天然支架Collagen 膠原蛋白 (crosslink glutaraldehyde), Gelatin 明膠 (hydrolysis), Hyaluronan 透明質酸 (acrylate), Fibrin 纖維蛋白 (thrombin), Alginate 藻酸鹽 (Ca), Chitosan 殼聚糖 (GAG-like).
Synthetic Scaffolds 合成支架PLA (brittle 脆), PGA (sutures 縫合線), PCL (slow degrade 慢降解), Injectables 可注射材料 (PPF-DA).
3D Cultures 3D培養Vs 2D: In vivo-like; Limitations: Diffusion; Apps: Regen med, bioreactors for transport. 對比2D：更似體內；局限性：擴散；應用：再生醫學，生物反應器用於物質傳輸。

Topic 1 – Introduction to Polymers

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Polymers are large molecules essential for tissue engineering scaffolds due to their versatility in mimicking ECM.
Mind map: Polymers → Natural (Wood/Rubber/DNA) → Synthetic (PE/PS/PEG) → Biomedical Suitability (Strength/Biodegradability/Fabrication) → Disadvantages (Deformation/Toxicity/Degradation).

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • Polymers revolutionized chemistry with major everyday impact (Lord Todd quote).
  • ECM components like collagen, proteins, and GAGs are polymers.
  • Suited for biomedical apps: low/high strength, protein attachment resistance/encouragement.
  • Biodegradable or permanent; fabricable into complex shapes.
  • Disadvantages: lower strength than metals/ceramics, deform over time.
  • May deteriorate during sterilization or degrade catastrophically in body.
  • Release toxic byproducts possible.
  • Natural examples: Wood, Rubber, Leather, Cotton, Wool, Silk, DNA, Proteins.
  • Synthetic examples: Polystyrene, PET, PE, PEG, Kevlar.
  • Polymers in scaffolds mimic ECM for cell support.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

Natural PolymersSynthetic Polymers
From nature (e.g., wood, DNA)Man-made (e.g., PE, PS)
Biocompatible but variableConsistent but may inflame
Often biodegradableTunable degradation

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Confusing polymers with monomers; trap: asking for polymer examples without specifying type.
  • Overlooking disadvantages like toxic byproducts in body.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • “Poly-Many”: Polymers are many monomers linked (poly = many).

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

What are the advantages of polymers in biomedical applications?

Versatile strength, protein attachment control, biodegradability, and easy fabrication into shapes.

Name two disadvantages of polymers.

Lower strength/deformation over time; potential toxic degradation products.

Topic 2 – Polymerization Types

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Polymerization forms chains via addition (non-degradable) or step-growth (degradable via hydrolysis).
Mind map: Polymerization → Addition (Radicals/Ions → No labile bonds) → Step-Growth (Condensation → Ester bonds → Hydrolysis) → Examples (PE vs PLA/PGA/PLGA).

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • Addition: Double-bond monomers like ethylene, vinyl chloride, styrene, MMA.
  • Via radicals or ions; no labile bonds, not degradable.
  • Example: Ethylene → Polyethylene.
  • Step-growth: Functional group reactions, condensation.
  • Well-known: PLA, PGA, PLGA.
  • Degrade via main chain ester hydrolysis.
  • PLA from lactic acid condensation.
  • PGA from glycolic acid.
  • PLGA copolymers tunable.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

Addition PolymerizationStep-Growth Polymerization
Double bonds breakFunctional groups react
Non-degradable chainsDegradable via hydrolysis
Examples: PE, PVCExamples: PLA, PGA

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Mixing addition and step-growth; trap: classify PLA as addition (it’s step-growth).
  • Forgetting condensation byproduct in step-growth.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • “Add No Water”: Addition has no H2O byproduct; “Step Condense”: Step-growth condenses with H2O loss.

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

Describe addition polymerization.

Involves double-bond monomers reacting via radicals/ions, forming non-degradable polymers.

Why do step-growth polymers degrade?

Hydrolysis of main chain ester bonds.

Topic 3 – Polymer Structures and States

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Polymers exist as crystalline, amorphous, or semi-crystalline, affecting melting and glass transitions.
Mind map: Structures → Crystals (Ordered/Lattice/Sharp Tm) → Amorphous (No order/Tg) → Polymers (Semi-crystalline/Folding/Stacking) → States (Rubber/Glass).

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • Crystals: Infinite 3D lattice, sharp Tm.
  • Amorphous: No structure, sharp Tm.
  • Small molecules: 100% crystalline or amorphous.
  • Polymer crystalline: Intramolecular folding/stacking.
  • Semi-crystalline: Never 100% crystalline, have Tm.
  • Rubber/Glass: Frozen liquid, no long-range order.
  • Tg: Below Tg, no cooperative rotation/translation.
  • Transition: Rubbery to glassy hard state.
  • Crystallization favored by simple structures, small side groups.
  • Linear, non-crosslinked polymers crystallize better.
  • Block/alternating copolymers, slow cooling aid crystallization.
  • Crystalline: Stronger, resistant to dissolution.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

CrystallineAmorphous
Ordered latticeNo order
Sharp TmTg only
StrongerFlexible

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Assuming polymers are 100% crystalline; trap: polymers are semi-crystalline.
  • Confusing Tm and Tg.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • “Crystal Clear Tm”: Crystals have sharp melting; “Amorphous Tg”: Amorphous has glass transition.

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

What is Tg?

Glass transition temperature where polymer shifts from rubbery to glassy.

Factors favoring crystallization?

Simple structures, linear chains, slow cooling.

Topic 4 – Polymer Properties

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Properties like crystallinity, thermal behavior, and side groups influence polymer performance in scaffolds.
Mind map: Properties → Crystallinity (Favored by simplicity) → Side Groups (Flexible lower Tg) → Thermal (Tg/Tm values) → Effects (Free volume/Motion).

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • Crystallization: Simple/small side groups, linear, non-network.
  • Slow cooling for chain motion.
  • Crystalline stronger/resistant.
  • Flexible side groups: Tg decreases with length.
  • Side chains rotate, increase free volume, lower Tg.
  • Thermal examples: Nylon 6,6 Tg 45°C Tm 267°C.
  • UHMWPE Tg -125°C Tm 140°C.
  • Silicone Tg -123°C Tm -29°C.
  • PMMA Tg 105°C Tm 160°C.
  • PDLLA Tg 50°C amorphous.
  • PCL Tg -60°C Tm 57°C.
  • PGA Tg 35°C Tm 210°C.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

Short Side GroupsLong Side Groups
Higher TgLower Tg
Less free volumeMore free volume
Restricted motionEasier rotation

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Thinking longer sides increase Tg; actually decrease.
  • Ignoring side chain effects on packing.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks
