Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

What is the role of adult stem cells?

Maintain homeostasis, replace lost cells via progenitors.

Compare HSCs and MSCs.

HSCs for blood; MSCs for connective tissues.

Topic 7 – Stem Cell Niche and Plasticity

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

The stem cell niche provides a protective environment balancing renewal and differentiation, while plasticity allows unexpected lineage switches.
Mind map:
Niche → Microenvironment → Deterministic (Spatial) vs Stochastic (Switch) → HSC (Bone/Vessels) → MSC (Perivascular) → Plasticity (Cross-Lineage)

Must-Know Facts (max 12 bullet points)

  • 🔴 Niche: shelters stem cells, balances quiescence/activity.
  • 🟢 Deterministic: asymmetric division by constraints.
  • 🔴 Stochastic: random symmetric/asymmetric switch.
  • 🟢 HSC niche: bone surfaces or sinusoids.
  • 🔵 MSC niche: perivascular, interactions via adhesion/ECM.
  • 🟢 Plasticity: adult SC differentiate beyond tissue (e.g., across germ layers).
  • 🔴 Vascular SC: from bovine artery, form bone in sclerosis.
  • 🟢 Hypothesis: stem cells cause aortic calcification.
  • 🔵 New research: transdifferentiation possible.
  • 🟢 Low frequency but significant for TE.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

Deterministic vs Stochastic NicheDeterministicStochastic
DivisionAlways asymmetricSwitches modes
ControlSpatial/functional constraintsProbabilistic
OutcomeRetained in nicheVariable pool size

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Assuming all niches are deterministic (trap: MSCs more stochastic).
  • Underestimating plasticity in adult SC.
  • Confusing niche with ECM alone.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • “Det Sto Niche” (Deterministic Stochastic).

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

Describe stem cell niche models.

Deterministic: spatial retention; Stochastic: random division modes.

What is plasticity?

Ability to differentiate across lineages.

Topic 8 – Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells (iPSCs)

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

iPSCs reprogram adult cells to pluripotent state using factors, offering autologous sources without ethical issues of ESCs.
Mind map:
iPSCs → Reprogramming (Oct4, Sox2, c-Myc, Klf4) → Retroviral Delivery → Characterization (Chimera, Teratoma) → Advantages (Unlimited, Autologous) → Limitations (Low Efficiency, Tumors)

Must-Know Facts (max 12 bullet points)

  • 🔴 Reprogram somatic cells to ESC-like.
  • 🟢 Factors: Oct4, Sox2 (pluripotency); c-Myc, Klf4 (phenotype/proliferation).
  • 🔴 Delivered via retrovirus.
  • 🟢 Mouse 2006, human 2007 (Yamanaka, Nobel 2012).
  • 🔵 Characterization: embryoid bodies, teratoma, chimera formation.
  • 🟢 Advantages: from adult tissues, unlimited, no ethics like ESCs.
  • 🔴 Limitations: low throughput (0.01-0.1%), mutations, oncogenes.
  • 🟢 Second gen: Nanog selection, chimeric mice.
  • 🔵 Risk: 20% chimeric mice develop cancer from c-Myc.
  • 🟢 From stomach, liver, skin, etc.
  • 🔴 Latest: synthetic embryos ex utero (2022).
  • 🟢 Autologous but potential rejection.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

ESCs vs iPSCsESCsiPSCs
SourceEmbryosAdult cells
EthicsMoral objectionsNone
EfficiencyHigh from blastocystLow reprogramming
RiskRejectionMutations, tumors

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Forgetting oncogenic risks of factors.
  • Assuming iPSCs are fully identical to ESCs (trap: epigenetic differences).
  • Overlooking low success rate.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • “OSCK Factors” (Oct4, Sox2, c-Myc, Klf4).

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

What factors induce iPSCs?

Oct4, Sox2, c-Myc, Klf4.

List limitations of iPSCs.

Low throughput, genomic insertions, tumor risk.

Active Recall Bank

  • What are the four components of tissue engineering? (____)
  • Name a limitation of differentiated cells. (____)
  • What is a primary culture? (____)
  • Define stemness. (____)
  • What is self-renewal? (____)
  • Explain intrinsic asymmetry. (____)
  • What is totipotency? (____)
  • How are ESCs isolated? (____)
  • Name an ESC marker. (____)
  • What are embryoid bodies? (____)
  • Role of adult stem cells? (____)
  • What is HSC niche? (____)
  • Define plasticity. (____)
  • What is deterministic niche model? (____)
  • Factors for iPSCs? (____)
  • Limitation of iPSCs? (____)
  • What is HeLa? (____)
  • Example of transdifferentiation. (____)
  • Potency levels: uni/multi/pluri/toti. (____)
  • Latest news on synthetic embryos. (____)

15-Minute Mock Exam

  1. Describe the tissue engineering paradigm.

    Integrates cells, materials, biochemical and biophysical factors for regeneration.

  2. List three limitations of differentiated cells.

    Limited availability, morbidity, low proliferation.

  3. What defines a cell line?

    Primary culture after subculture.

  4. Explain stem cell self-renewal.

    Division maintaining stem phenotype, symmetric or asymmetric.

  5. How are ESCs cultured?

    On mouse feeders with bFGF for humans.

  6. What is the role of stem cell niche?

    Balances quiescence and differentiation.

  7. Describe iPSC reprogramming.

    Using Oct4, Sox2, c-Myc, Klf4 via retrovirus.

  8. Compare ESCs and iPSCs.

    ESCs from embryos (ethical issues); iPSCs from adults (low efficiency).

One-Page Cheat Sheet Summary

Key ConceptsDetails
TE ParadigmCells + Materials + Biochemical + Biophysical
Cell TypesDifferentiated (limited) vs Stem (renew/diff)
CulturesPrimary → Lines (finite/immortal e.g., HeLa)
Stem FundamentalsSelf-renewal (asym/sym), Differentiation (progenitors)
PotencyToti (all + extra) > Pluri (3 layers) > Multi > Uni
ESCsBlastocyst, feeders, markers (Oct4), applications (neurons)
Adult SCHSC/MSC, niche (perivascular), plasticity
iPSCsReprogram factors (OSCK), chimeric, risks (tumors)
entry.summary: <h1 id="BME2104-Tissue-Engineering-–-2"><a href="#BME2104-Tissue-Engineering-–-2" class="headerlink" title="BME2104 Tissue Engineering – 2">

entry.title: BME 2104 Tissue Engineering – 4
entry.content:
BME 2104 Tissue Engineering – 4

Lecture Overview

  • Top 8 topics that appear every year: Fibronectin and RGD Binding, Focal Adhesions in 2D vs 3D, Cell Migration and Chemotaxis, Signaling Ligands and Types, Central Paradigm of Signal Transduction, G-Protein Coupled Receptors, Receptor Tyrosine Kinases and Pathways, Mechanotransduction and Cytoskeleton
  • Colour code: 🔴 Memorise | 🟢 Understand | 🔵 Apply
  • 每年必出嘅八大題目 (Top 8 topics that appear every year):
    1. Fibronectin 纖連蛋白 同 RGD Binding 結合
    2. Focal Adhesions 黏著斑 in 2D vs 3D
    3. Cell Migration 細胞遷移 同 Chemotaxis 趨化性
    4. Signaling Ligands 信號配體 同 Types 類型
    5. Central Paradigm of Signal Transduction 信號轉導中心法則
    6. G-Protein Coupled Receptors (GPCRs) G蛋白偶聯受體
    7. Receptor Tyrosine Kinases (RTKs) 受體酪氨酸激酶 同 Pathways 信號途徑
    8. Mechanotransduction 機械轉導 同 Cytoskeleton 細胞骨架
  • 顏色標記 (Colour code): 🔴 Memorise 必須背誦 | 🟢 Understand 必須理解 | 🔵 Apply 必須應用

Topic 1 – Fibronectin 纖連蛋白 同 Integrin 整合素 Binding 結合

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Fibronectin 纖連蛋白係關鍵嘅 ECM (Extracellular Matrix) 細胞外基質 glycoprotein 糖蛋白，透過 integrins 整合素促進 cell adhesion 細胞黏附，對組織結構同信號傳遞至關重要。

思維導圖 (Mind map):
Fibronectin → Types 類型 (Soluble Plasma 可溶性血漿型 → Bloodstream 血液循環 → Hepatocytes 肝細胞; Insoluble Cellular 不可溶性細胞型 → Secreted by Fibroblasts 成纖維細胞分泌) → Binds to 結合對象 (Integrins 整合素 → RGD Sequence RGD序列 → α5β1/αVβ3; ECM Components ECM成分 → Collagen 膠原蛋白/Fibrin 纖維蛋白/Heparan Sulfate 硫酸肝素) → Enables Cell Attachment 實現細胞黏附。

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • 🔴 Fibronectin 纖連蛋白係結合 integrins 整合素嘅主要 ECM 糖蛋白。
  • 🟢 亦結合其他 ECM 成分，如 collagen 膠原蛋白、fibrin 纖維蛋白、heparan sulfate proteoglycans 硫酸肝素蛋白聚糖。
  • 🔴 兩種類型 (Two types): soluble plasma 可溶性血漿型 (hepatocytes 肝細胞產生) 同 insoluble cellular 不可溶性細胞型 (fibroblasts 成纖維細胞等分泌)。
  • 🟢 Integrin 整合素 binding 結合使用 RGD sequence RGD序列 (Arg-Gly-Asp 精氨酸-甘氨酸-天冬氨酸)。
  • 🔴 關鍵 integrins 整合素: α5β1 同 αVβ3 (喺細胞表面)。
  • 🟢 短嘅 RGD peptides RGD肽可以模仿 fibronectin 纖連蛋白功能，促進 cell attachment 細胞黏附。
  • 🔵 RGD 序列促成 focal adhesion 黏著斑形成。
  • 🟢 Plasma fibronectin 血漿纖連蛋白喺血液中循環；cellular fibronectin 細胞型纖連蛋白則整合入 matrix 基質。
  • 🔴 RGD 係 primary cell attachment site 主要細胞黏附位點。
  • 🟢 Binding 結合促進 clustering 聚集，形成 focal adhesions 黏著斑。
  • 🔵 應用於 biomaterials 生物材料，用於 cell adhesion engineering 細胞黏附工程。
  • 🟢 Insoluble form 不可溶性型由好多細胞分泌，例如 fibroblasts 成纖維細胞。
  • 🔴 Heparan sulfate 硫酸肝素 binding 結合有助於 growth factor 生長因子 localization 定位。
  • 🟢 Fibronectin 纖連蛋白 domains 結構域可以結合 TGF-β 同 bFGF。

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - Soluble Plasma vs Insoluble Cellular Fibronectin 可溶性血漿型 vs 不可溶性細胞型纖連蛋白

Aspect 方面Soluble Plasma Fibronectin (X) 可溶性血漿型纖連蛋白Insoluble Cellular Fibronectin (Y) 不可溶性細胞型纖連蛋白
Source 來源Produced by hepatocytes 肝細胞產生Secreted by fibroblasts etc. 成纖維細胞等分泌
Location 位置Circulates in bloodstream 喺血液循環中Integrated into ECM 整合入細胞外基質
Function 功能Coagulation 凝血, wound healing 傷口癒合Cell adhesion 細胞黏附, matrix structure 基質結構
Solubility 溶解性Soluble 可溶性Insoluble 不可溶性
Production 生產Liver-specific 肝臟特異性Various cell types 多種細胞類型

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 誤以為 RGD 係唯一結合位點 (陷阱: 忘記其他協同區域，例如 PHSRN)。
  • 混淆 fibronectin 纖連蛋白嘅類型: 以為所有纖連蛋白都係 insoluble 不可溶性，喺 ECM 入面。
  • 忽略 RGD 喺 tissue engineering (TE) 組織工程應用中，用於 synthetic peptides 合成肽嘅作用。

記憶法 (Mnemonics / Memory Hooks)

  • RGD: “Really Good Dance” – Arg-Gly-Asp 精氨酸-甘氨酸-天冬氨酸令細胞”跳舞” (黏附同移動)。
  • Fibronectin types 纖連蛋白類型: “Plasma Soluble in Blood, Cellular Insoluble in Matrix” – PSB-CIM。

過往試題 + 標準答案 (Past Exam Questions + Model Answers)

What is the role of the RGD sequence in fibronectin? 纖連蛋白中 RGD 序列嘅作用係咩？

The RGD (Arg-Gly-Asp) sequence is the primary integrin-binding site facilitating cell attachment via α5β1 and αVβ3 integrins.
RGD (精氨酸-甘氨酸-天冬氨酸) 序列係主要嘅整合素結合位點，透過 α5β1 同 αVβ3 整合素促進細胞黏附。

Compare soluble and insoluble fibronectin. 比較可溶性同不可溶性纖連蛋白。

Soluble plasma fibronectin circulates in blood from hepatocytes for systemic roles; insoluble cellular fibronectin is secreted locally for ECM integration and adhesion.
可溶性血漿型纖連蛋白由肝細胞產生，喺血液中循環，發揮全身性作用；不可溶性細胞型纖連蛋白由局部細胞分泌，用於 ECM 整合同黏附。

Topic 2 – Focal Adhesions 黏著斑

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Focal adhesions 黏著斑係 integrin 整合素介導嘅聚集結構，連結 ECM 細胞外基質 同 cytoskeleton 細胞骨架，傳遞信號同機械力。
思維導圖 (Mind map):
Focal Adhesion 黏著斑 → Integrin Binds 整合素結合 (Collagen 膠原蛋白/Fibronectin 纖連蛋白 → RGD) → Cluster Formation 聚集形成 → Components 組成部分 (Vinculin/Talin/FAK/Actin/Myosin) → Signals to Nucleus 信號傳至細胞核 → 2D (Punctate 點狀) vs 3D (Diffuse 瀰漫性).

必須掌握嘅要點 (Must-Know Facts)

  • 🔴 Integrins 整合素 bind 結合 ECM (如 collagen 膠原蛋白/fibronectin 纖連蛋白) 形成 focal adhesions 黏著斑。
  • 🟢 Focal adhesions 黏著斑係細胞黏附聚集結構。
  • 🔴 顯微成像中常用綠色螢光標記 focal adhesions 黏著斑。
  • 🟢 2D 環境下嘅 adhesions 黏著斑呈 punctate 點狀；3D 環境下呈 fibrillar/diffuse 纖維狀/瀰漫性。
  • 🔵 透過 cytoskeleton 細胞骨架將 mechanical signals 機械信號傳遞至 nucleus 細胞核。
  • 🟢 涉及蛋白質: vinculin, talin, paxillin, FAK。
  • 🔴 Neural progenitor cells (NPCs) 神經祖細胞: 喺 3D 環境中，remodeling 重塑 > binding 結合。
  • 🟢 NPC stemness NPC幹性對 3D 環境中嘅 adhesive ligands 黏附配體不敏感。
  • 🔵 應用於 TE 組織工程，用於模仿 ECM 嘅 scaffold 支架設計。
  • 🟢 機械力來自 actin-myosin contraction 肌動蛋白-肌球蛋白收縮。
  • 🔴 Turnover 更新調節 migration 遷移。
  • 🟢 FAK 透過 phosphorylation 磷酸化進行信號傳遞。
  • 🔵 3D 環境會改變 adhesion morphology 黏附形態。

關鍵公式/圖表 (Key Formulas / Diagrams)

對比表 (Comparison Table) - 2D vs 3D Focal Adhesions

Aspect 方面2D Focal Adhesions (X)3D Focal Adhesions (Y)
Morphology 形態Punctate 點狀, discrete 離散Fibrillar 纖維狀, diffuse 瀰漫性
Environment 環境Flat surfaces 平坦表面Volumetric matrices 立體基質
Function 功能Strong anchorage 強力錨定Dynamic remodeling 動態重塑
Imaging 成像Bright spots 明亮點狀Linear extensions 線性延伸
NPC Response 神經祖細胞反應Ligand-sensitive 配體敏感Remodeling-focused 聚焦重塑

常見錯誤同考官陷阱 (Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps)

  • 假設 2D 同 3D 嘅 adhesions 黏著斑係一樣嘅 (陷阱: 喺 TE 設計中忽略維度影響)。
  • 忘記 focal adhesions 黏著斑除咗黏附，仲有 signaling 信號傳遞嘅作用。
  • 忽略 NPC 神經祖細胞喺 3D 中對 ligands 配體不敏感呢點。

記憶法 (Mnemonics / Memory Hooks)

  • Focal Adhesion 黏著斑: “FACTS” – Fibronectin-Actin Connection Transmits Signals 纖連蛋白-肌動蛋白連接傳遞信號。
  • 2D vs 3D: “Punctate in Plane (2D), Fibrils in Full (3D)” 平面上點狀(2D)，立體中纖維(3D)。

過往試題 + 標準答案 (Past Exam Questions + Model Answers)

Describe focal adhesion formation. 描述黏著斑嘅形成。

Integrins bind ECM (e.g., fibronectin via RGD), clustering with talin/vinculin/actin to form adhesions transmitting signals.
整合素結合 ECM (例如，透過 RGD 結合纖連蛋白)，與 talin/vinculin/肌動蛋白聚集形成黏著斑，傳遞信號。

How do focal adhesions differ in 2D vs 3D? 黏著斑喺 2D 同 3D 環境中有咩唔同？

In 2D, they are punctate for strong anchorage; in 3D, fibrillar for remodeling, as seen in NPC maintenance.
喺 2D 環境，佢哋呈點狀用於強力錨定；喺 3D 環境，呈纖維狀用於重塑，正如喺 NPC 維持中所見。

(Topic 3-8 及以下部分因篇幅限制，將以同樣模式呈現關鍵詞中英對照，但內容精簡。)

Topic 3 – Cell Migration 細胞遷移 同 Chemotaxis 趨化性

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Cell migration 細胞遷移涉及 cytoskeleton 細胞骨架重組以進行移動，由 taxis 趨向性引導，例如 development 發育同 repair 修復中嘅 chemotaxis 趨化性。
Key Terms: Actin polymerization 肌動蛋白聚合, Myosin 肌球蛋白, Haptotaxis 趨觸性, Phototaxis 趨光性, Embryogenesis 胚胎發生。

Topic 4 – Signaling Ligands 信號配體 同 Types 類型

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Ligands 配體啟動 signaling 信號以協調組織發育，根據類型、作用範圍同功能分類。
Key Terms: Diffusible 可擴散, ECM-bound 細胞外基質結合, Endocrine 內分泌, Paracrine 旁分泌, Autocrine 自分泌, Juxtacrine 近分泌/並列分泌, Growth factors (GFs) 生長因子, Cytokines 細胞因子, Chemokines 趨化因子.

Topic 5 – Central Paradigm of Signal Transduction 信號轉導中心法則

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Signal transduction 信號轉導將 extracellular 細胞外 ligands 配體轉化為 intracellular 細胞內 responses 反應，透過 receptors 受體同 cascades 級聯反應，最終導致 gene activation 基因激活。
Key Terms: Initiation 啟動, Transduction 轉導, Transcription factors (TFs) 轉錄因子, Second messengers 第二信使, Translation 翻譯, mRNA vaccines mRNA疫苗。

Topic 6 – G-Protein Coupled Receptors (GPCRs) G蛋白偶聯受體

大圖景 (Big Picture)

GPCRs 透過 G-protein G蛋白解離傳遞多樣化信號，參與 sensory perception 感官知覺同生理調節。
Key Terms: 7 transmembrane (7TM) segments 7次跨膜片段, Agonist 激動劑, G-protein subunits (α/β/γ) G蛋白亞基, Effectors 效應器, Adenylate cyclase 腺苷酸環化酶, Phospholipase C (PLC) 磷脂酶C。

Topic 7 – Receptor Tyrosine Kinases (RTKs) 受體酪氨酸激酶 同 Pathways 信號途徑

大圖景 (Big Picture)

RTKs 喺 ligand 配體結合後 dimerize 二聚化並發生 phosphorylation 磷酸化，啟動 cascades 級聯反應，參與生長同 angiogenesis 血管生成。
Key Terms: Dimerization 二聚化, Autophosphorylation 自磷酸化, Ras/Raf/MEK/ERK pathway, TGF-β (Transforming Growth Factor-β) 轉化生長因子-β, Smad proteins Smad蛋白, Wnt pathway, β-catenin β-連環蛋白。

Topic 8 – Mechanotransduction 機械轉導 同 Cytoskeleton 細胞骨架

大圖景 (Big Picture)

Mechanotransduction 機械轉導將 physical forces 物理力轉化為 biochemical responses 生化反應，透過 cytoskeleton 細胞骨架實現，對骨同血管健康至關重要。
Key Terms: Shear stress 剪切應力, Tension 張力, Actin filaments (Microfilaments) 肌動蛋白絲 (微絲), Microtubules (MTs) 微管, Intermediate filaments (IFs) 中間絲, Treadmilling 踏車運動, Dynamic instability 動態不穩定性, Stress shielding 應力遮擋。

Active Recall Bank 主動回憶庫

  • What sequence in fibronectin binds integrins? (RGD)
  • Name two types of fibronectin. (Soluble plasma, insoluble cellular)
  • Focal adhesions in 2D are ____; in 3D ____. (punctate, fibrillar)
  • Chemotaxis responds to ____ gradients. (chemical)
  • Ligand range: distant via blood? (Endocrine)
  • Central paradigm steps: Initiation, ____, Gene Activation. (Transduction)
  • GPCRs have ____ transmembrane segments. (7)
  • RTKs phosphorylate ____ residues. (tyrosine)
  • Cytoskeleton filaments: Actin, ____, Intermediate. (Microtubules)
  • Treadmilling occurs in ____ filaments. (actin)
  • BMP signaling uses ____ proteins. (Smads)
  • Wnt canonical pathway stabilizes ____. (β-catenin)
  • Stress shielding causes bone ____. (atrophy 萎縮)
  • Myosin uses ____ for force. (ATP)
  • MTOC (Microtubule Organizing Center) 微管組織中心 organizes ____. (microtubules 微管)
  • RGD stands for --____. (Arg-Gly-Asp)
  • Taxis type for light: ____. (Phototaxis 趨光性)
  • Translation starts with codon ____. (AUG)
  • Ion channels can be ____-gated or ____-gated. (voltage 電壓, ligand 配體)
  • Paclitaxel ____ microtubules. (stabilizes 穩定)

15-Minute Mock Exam 15分鐘模擬考試

  1. Explain the role of RGD in cell adhesion and its TE applications.

    RGD (Arg-Gly-Asp) is the integrin-binding site in fibronectin promoting attachment; in TE, short RGD peptides functionalize scaffolds for cell adhesion without full proteins.

  2. Compare focal adhesions in 2D vs 3D environments, with NPC example.

    2D: punctate, ligand-sensitive; 3D: fibrillar, remodeling-focused; for NPCs, 3D stemness maintenance is insensitive to ligands, prioritizing remodeling.

  3. Describe chemotaxis and its measurement.

    Directed migration to chemical gradients; assessed by polarized morphology and proportion of cells moving toward/away from attractant, depending on concentration and gradient steepness.

  4. Outline the central signal transduction paradigm.

    Initiation: ligand-receptor binding; Transduction: cascade activates nuclear TF; Gene activation: TF binds DNA, leading to transcription, translation, and physiological changes.

  5. How does Wnt canonical pathway work?

    Ligand binds Fz/LRP, stabilizes β-catenin, which enters nucleus and interacts with TCF/Lef for gene transcription in development and cancer.

One-Page Cheat Sheet Summary 一頁速查表總結

Section 部分Key Points 要點 (中英對照)
ECM/Adhesion 細胞外基質/黏附Fibronectin 纖連蛋白: RGD binds α5β1/αVβ3; Focal adhesions 黏著斑: 2D punctate vs 3D fibrillar; NPCs 神經祖細胞: remodeling 重塑 > binding 結合 in 3D.
Migration 遷移Cycle 循環: Attach-Contract-Detach 黏附-收縮-脫離; Taxis 趨向性: Chemo 趨化性 (gradients 梯度), Hapto 趨觸性 (adhesives 黏附分子); Defects 缺陷: embryonic issues 胚胎問題.
Signaling 信號傳導Ligands 配體: Endocrine內分泌/Paracrine旁分泌/Autocrine自分泌/Juxtacrine近分泌; GFs 生長因子: VEGF/TGF-β; Paradigm 法則: Bind結合 → Cascade級聯 → Gene基因.
Receptors 受體GPCR: 7TM, dissociate G解離G蛋白; RTK: Dimer二聚/P-Tyr酪氨酸磷酸化/Ras-ERK通路; TGF-β: Ser/Thr絲氨酸/蘇氨酸/Smads; Wnt: β-catenin/TCF.
Transport 運輸Hydrophobic 疏水性: Diffuse擴散 (NO/AA); Ion Channels 離子通道: Voltage/Ligand gated電壓/配體門控 (Ca2+/Na+); Optogenetics 光遺傳學: Light control光控制.
Cytoskeleton 細胞骨架Actin 肌動蛋白: Polymer聚合/Treadmill踏車運動/Myosin肌球蛋白; MT微管: Dynamic動態/GTP/Paclitaxel紫杉醇; Mechanotransduction機械轉導: Forces力 → Signals信號 (Wolff’s Law沃爾夫定律).
TE Apps 組織工程應用Dermal matrix 真皮基質: Cell invasion細胞侵入; Growth factors 生長因子 in scaffolds支架; Avoid stress shielding 避免應力遮擋 in implants植入物.

Topic 1 – Fibronectin and Integrin Binding

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Fibronectin is a key ECM glycoprotein facilitating cell adhesion via integrins, crucial for tissue structure and signaling.
Mind map: Fibronectin → Types (Soluble Plasma → Bloodstream → Hepatocytes; Insoluble Cellular → Secreted by Fibroblasts) → Binds to (Integrins → RGD Sequence → α5β1/αVβ3; ECM Components → Collagen/Fibrin/Heparan Sulfate) → Enables Cell Attachment.

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • 🔴 Fibronectin is the major ECM glycoprotein binding integrins.
  • 🟢 Binds other ECM like collagen, fibrin, heparan sulfate proteoglycans.
  • 🔴 Two types: soluble plasma (hepatocytes) and insoluble cellular (fibroblasts).
  • 🟢 Integrin binding uses RGD sequence (Arg-Gly-Asp).
  • 🔴 Key integrins: α5β1 and αVβ3 on cell surface.
  • 🟢 Short RGD peptides can mimic fibronectin for cell attachment.
  • 🔵 RGD enables focal adhesion formation.
  • 🟢 Plasma fibronectin circulates in blood; cellular is matrix-integrated.
  • 🔴 RGD is the primary cell attachment site.
  • 🟢 Binding promotes clustering into focal adhesions.
  • 🔵 Used in biomaterials for cell adhesion engineering.
  • 🟢 Insoluble form secreted by many cells like fibroblasts.
  • 🔴 Heparan sulfate binding aids in growth factor localization.
  • 🟢 Fibronectin domains bind TGF-β and bFGF actively.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

AspectSoluble Plasma Fibronectin (X)Insoluble Cellular Fibronectin (Y)
SourceProduced by hepatocytesSecreted by fibroblasts etc.
LocationCirculates in bloodstreamIntegrated into ECM
FunctionCoagulation, wound healingCell adhesion, matrix structure
SolubilitySolubleInsoluble
ProductionLiver-specificVarious cell types

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Mistaking RGD as the only binding site (trap: forget synergy regions like PHSRN).
  • Confusing fibronectin types: assuming all fibronectin is insoluble in ECM.
  • Overlooking RGD’s role in synthetic peptides for TE applications.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • RGD: “Really Good Dance” – Arg-Gly-Asp makes cells “dance” (attach and move).
  • Fibronectin types: “Plasma Soluble in Blood, Cellular Insoluble in Matrix” – PSB-CIM.

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

What is the role of the RGD sequence in fibronectin?

The RGD (Arg-Gly-Asp) sequence is the primary integrin-binding site facilitating cell attachment via α5β1 and αVβ3 integrins.

Compare soluble and insoluble fibronectin.

Soluble plasma fibronectin circulates in blood from hepatocytes for systemic roles; insoluble cellular fibronectin is secreted locally for ECM integration and adhesion.

Topic 2 – Focal Adhesions

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Focal adhesions are integrin-mediated clusters linking ECM to cytoskeleton, transmitting signals and forces.
Mind map: Focal Adhesion → Integrin Binds (Collagen/Fibronectin → RGD) → Cluster Formation → Components (Vinculin/Talin/FAK/Actin/Myosin) → Signals to Nucleus → 2D (Punctate) vs 3D (Diffuse).

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • 🔴 Integrins bind ECM like collagen/fibronectin to form focal adhesions.
  • 🟢 Focal adhesions are cell adhesion clusters.
  • 🔴 Green fluorescence often labels focal adhesions in imaging.
  • 🟢 2D adhesions are punctate; 3D are fibrillar/diffuse.
  • 🔵 Transmit mechanical signals to nucleus via cytoskeleton.
  • 🟢 Involve proteins: vinculin, talin, paxillin, FAK.
  • 🔴 Neural progenitor cells: remodeling > binding in 3D.
  • 🟢 NPC stemness insensitive to adhesive ligands in 3D.
  • 🔵 Used in TE for scaffold design mimicking ECM.
  • 🟢 Forces from actin-myosin contraction.
  • 🔴 Turnover regulates migration.
  • 🟢 FAK phosphorylates for signaling.
  • 🔵 3D environments alter adhesion morphology.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

Aspect2D Focal Adhesions (X)3D Focal Adhesions (Y)
MorphologyPunctate, discreteFibrillar, diffuse
EnvironmentFlat surfacesVolumetric matrices
FunctionStrong anchorageDynamic remodeling
ImagingBright spotsLinear extensions
NPC ResponseLigand-sensitiveRemodeling-focused

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Assuming 2D and 3D adhesions are identical (trap: ignore dimensionality in TE designs).
  • Forgetting focal adhesions’ role in signaling, not just adhesion.
  • Overlooking NPC insensitivity to ligands in 3D.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • Focal Adhesion: “FACTS” – Fibronectin-Actin Connection Transmits Signals.
  • 2D vs 3D: “Punctate in Plane (2D), Fibrils in Full (3D)”.

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

Describe focal adhesion formation.

Integrins bind ECM (e.g., fibronectin via RGD), clustering with talin/vinculin/actin to form adhesions transmitting signals.

How do focal adhesions differ in 2D vs 3D?

In 2D, they are punctate for strong anchorage; in 3D, fibrillar for remodeling, as seen in NPC maintenance.

Topic 3 – Cell Migration and Chemotaxis

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Cell migration involves cytoskeleton reorganization for movement, guided by taxis like chemotaxis in development and repair.
Mind map: Migration → Mechanisms (Attachment → Contraction → Detachment) → Cytoskeleton (Actin Polymerization → Myosin Force) → Taxis (Chemo → Gradient; Hapto → Adhesive; Photo → Light) → Applications (Embryogenesis → Wound Healing).

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • 🔴 Cells migrate via attachment, contraction, detachment.
  • 🟢 Not just elastic bags; involve cytoskeleton reorganization.
  • 🔴 Chemotaxis: movement by chemical gradients (e.g., cAMP).
  • 🟢 Polarized morphology in migrating cells.
  • 🔵 Strength depends on concentration and gradient steepness.
  • 🟢 Taxis types: topo (turning to stimulus), klino (less turning oriented), ortho (speed increase oriented).
  • 🔴 Defects cause embryonic malformations, epilepsy.
  • 🟢 In skin repair: cells invade dermal matrix.
  • 🔵 Actin polymerization initiates; myosin maintains.
  • 🟢 Focal adhesions as traction points.
  • 🔴 Neutrophil chases bacterium via chemotaxis.
  • 🟢 Cilia motion for fluid movement.
  • 🔵 Embryonic: blastocyst implantation requires migration.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

AspectChemotaxis (X)Haptotaxis (Y)
StimulusDissolved chemicalsInsoluble adhesives
MechanismGradient sensingAdhesive gradient
ExampleNeutrophil to bacteriaCell on ECM gradient
ResponseDirected speed/turningEnhanced adhesion-move
TE ApplicationWound healing signalsScaffold design

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Confusing chemotaxis with random migration (trap: ignore gradient dependency).
  • Forgetting migration’s role in embryogenesis defects.
  • Overlooking contraction’s myosin dependence.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • Taxis Types: “TACO” – Topo/ortho (Turn/Accelerate), Chemo/Hapto (Chemical/Adhesive Orientation).
  • Migration: “ACD” – Attach-Contract-Detach.

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

Explain chemotaxis.

Directed migration responding to chemical gradients, with polarized cells moving toward higher concentrations.

Why is cell migration crucial in embryonic development?

Defects lead to implantation failure, malformed tissues, or congenital abnormalities like epilepsy.

Topic 4 – Signaling Ligands and Types

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Ligands initiate signaling for coordination in tissue development, classified by type, range, and function.
Mind map: Ligands → Molecular (Diffusible → Growth Factors/Steroids; ECM → Proteoglycans/Collagen) → Range (Endocrine → Distant; Paracrine → Nearby; Autocrine → Self; Juxtacrine → Contact) → Function (Growth Factors → VEGF/TGF-β; Hormones/Cytokines/Chemokines).

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • 🔴 Ligands: diffusible (soluble proteins, steroids) or ECM-bound.
  • 🟢 Range: endocrine (distant, blood), paracrine (local), autocrine (self), juxtacrine (contact).
  • 🔴 Growth factors stimulate proliferation/differentiation (VEGF, TGF-β, bFGF, EGF).
  • 🟢 Hormones: messengers via circulation.
  • 🔵 Cytokines affect other cells; chemokines are chemotactic.
  • 🟢 Matrikines: ECM fragments with activity.
  • 🔴 Thrombospondins inhibit angiogenesis.
  • 🟢 Signaling: ligand generation → response (proliferation, migration, ECM production).
  • 🔵 TE applications: growth factors in scaffolds.
  • 🟢 Parathyroid hormone: endocrine for bone remodeling.
  • 🔴 Interleukins: paracrine in skin.
  • 🟢 Autocrine: self-response.
  • 🔵 Juxtacrine requires cell-cell contact.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

AspectEndocrine Ligands (X)Paracrine Ligands (Y)
RangeDistant targetsNearby cells
TransportBlood/extracellularDiffusion
ExampleParathyroid hormoneInterleukins
SpeedSlower (systemic)Faster (local)
TE UseSystemic deliveryLocal scaffold release

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Mixing range with function (trap: call growth factors hormones).
  • Forgetting juxtacrine needs physical contact.
  • Assuming all ligands are soluble.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • Ranges: “EPAJ” – Endocrine-Paracrine-Autocrine-Juxtacrine (Every Person Acts Justly).
  • Growth Factors: “Very Tall Big Elephants” – VEGF/TGF-β/bFGF/EGF.

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

Classify ligands by range.

Endocrine: distant via blood; paracrine: local; autocrine: self; juxtacrine: contact-based.

Name key growth factors in TE.

VEGF (angiogenesis), TGF-β (differentiation), bFGF (proliferation), EGF (epithelial growth).

Topic 5 – Central Paradigm of Signal Transduction

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Signal transduction converts extracellular ligands into intracellular responses via receptors and cascades leading to gene activation.
Mind map: Paradigm → Initiation (Ligand Bind Receptor → Activation) → Transduction (Cascade → TF Activation) → Gene (TF Binds → Transcription → Protein Synthesis).

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • 🔴 Initiation: ligand binds membrane receptor, changes activity.
  • 🟢 Transduction: activates cascade to nuclear TF.
  • 🔴 Gene activation: TF binds regulatory sequences → mRNA/protein.
  • 🟢 TF: DNA-binding proteins; may translocate or activate.
  • 🔵 Involves second messengers (cAMP, Ca2+, NO).
  • 🟢 Transcription: RNA polymerase uses rNTPs.
  • 🔴 Translation: mRNA → protein via ribosomes, start AUG.
  • 🟢 Protein folding: ER → Golgi → secretion.
  • 🔵 mRNA vaccines: modified bases reduce inflammation.
  • 🟢 Protein structure: primary (AA sequence) to quaternary.
  • 🔴 Central dogma: signal → transduction → gene expression.
  • 🟢 Relocalization: e.g., NFκB from cytosol to nucleus.
  • 🔵 Phosphorylation common in cascades.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

AspectTranscription (X)Translation (Y)
LocationNucleusCytoplasm/Ribosomes
InputDNAmRNA
OutputmRNAProtein
Building BlocksrNTPsAmino Acids
InitiationPromoter/TFStart Codon AUG

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Confusing transcription with translation (trap: mix RNA vs protein synthesis).
  • Forgetting second messengers in transduction.
  • Overlooking protein processing post-translation.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • Paradigm: “I-T-G” – Initiation-Transduction-Gene Activation.
  • Codons: “AUG Starts Translation” – Always Use Gloves (safety hook).

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

Outline the central paradigm.

Ligand binds receptor (initiation), triggers cascade (transduction), activates TF for gene expression.

What is gene activation?

TF binds DNA regulatory sequences, increasing transcription rate to produce mRNA and proteins.

Topic 6 – G-Protein Coupled Receptors

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

GPCRs transmit diverse signals via G-protein dissociation, involved in senses and regulation.
Mind map: GPCR → 7 Transmembrane → Ligand Bind → G-Protein (α/β/γ Dissociate) → Effectors (Adenylate Cyclase/PLC) → Second Messengers (cAMP/IP3/Ca2+).

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • 🔴 GPCRs: 7 transmembrane segments.
  • 🟢 Respond to photons, amines, hormones, proteins.
  • 🔴 Agonist binding dissociates G-protein subunits.
  • 🟢 α subunit or β/γ interact with effectors.
  • 🔵 Involved in vision (photoisomerization), smell, immunity.
  • 🟢 Autonomic NS: blood pressure, heart rate.
  • 🔴 Visual: rhodopsin activation.
  • 🟢 Smell: olfactory receptors.
  • 🔵 Immune: chemokine receptors.
  • 🟢 Dissociation: GDP → GTP exchange.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

AspectBefore Binding (X)After Binding (Y)
G-Protein StateBound (αβγ)Dissociated (α + βγ)
NucleotideGDPGTP
EffectInactiveEffector Activation
ExampleResting ReceptorSignal Transduction

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Forgetting 7 TM structure (trap: confuse with RTKs).
  • Assuming all ligands bind extracellularly.
  • Overlooking β/γ subunit roles.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • GPCR: “7 Seas” – 7 Transmembrane for Signaling Seas (diverse ligands).
  • Dissociation: “Agonist Kicks GDP Out for GTP Party”.

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

Describe GPCR activation.

Ligand binds, causing G-protein interaction and dissociation of α (GTP-bound) from βγ.

GPCRs’ roles?

Vision (photons), smell (odorants), immunity (chemokines), autonomic regulation.

Topic 7 – Receptor Tyrosine Kinases and Pathways

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

RTKs dimerize upon ligand binding, phosphorylating for cascades in growth and angiogenesis.
Mind map: RTK → Ligand (e.g., VEGF) → Dimerization → Phosphorylation → Cascade (Ras/Raf/MEK/ERK) → TF (Ets-1) → Gene Expression.

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • 🔴 RTKs: dimerize, autophosphorylate tyrosines.
  • 🟢 Include VEGF-R, PDGF-R for vessel assembly.
  • 🔴 VEGF: endothelial differentiation.
  • 🟢 PDGF-B: recruits mural cells.
  • 🔵 Cascade: Sos → Ras → Raf → MEK → ERK → Nucleus.
  • 🟢 TGF-β: serine/threonine kinases, Smad pathway.
  • 🔴 BMPs: bone induction via Smads.
  • 🟢 Wnt: canonical (β-catenin → TCF/Lef), noncanonical (polarity/Ca2+).
  • 🔵 Involved in cancer, development.
  • 🟢 LRP5/6 co-receptors in Wnt.
  • 🔴 Fz: GPCR-like in Wnt.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

AspectRTK (X)Ser/Thr Kinase (Y)
PhosphorylationTyrosineSerine/Threonine
ExampleVEGF-RTGF-β/BMP R
DownstreamRas/MAPKSmads
FunctionGrowth/AngiogenesisDifferentiation/Bone

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Confusing Tyr vs Ser/Thr kinases (trap: mix VEGF with BMP).
  • Forgetting dimerization in RTKs.
  • Overlooking Wnt’s multiple pathways.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • RTK: “Dimer-Phos-Cascade” – Dinner Party Cascade (dimerize to start).
  • Wnt: “Beta Cat in Canon” – β-Catenin Canonical.

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

Outline VEGF signaling.

VEGF binds VEGF-R2, activates Ras via Sos, leading to ERK phosphorylation and Ets-1 activation for angiogenic genes.

Describe BMP pathway.

BMP binds receptors, phosphorylates Smad1/5/8, complexes with Smad4, translocates to nucleus for transcription.

Topic 8 – Mechanotransduction and Cytoskeleton

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Mechanotransduction converts physical forces into biochemical responses via cytoskeleton, vital for bone and vessel health.
Mind map: Forces (Shear/Tension) → Sense (Integrins/Junctions) → Transmit (Cytoskeleton → Actin/MT/IF) → Response (Gene Expression) → Applications (Stress Shielding/Osteoporosis).

Must-Know Facts (max 15 bullet points)

  • 🔴 Mechanotransduction: physical stimulus → physiological change.
  • 🟢 Cytoskeleton: actin (microfilaments), microtubules, intermediate filaments.
  • 🔴 Actin: G-actin → F-actin polymerization; (+) end growth.
  • 🟢 Myosin: ATP → force for contraction.
  • 🔵 Treadmilling: add at (+), remove at (-).
  • 🟢 Microtubules: α/β tubulin; dynamic instability.
  • 🔴 MTOC organizes polarity.
  • 🟢 Stress shielding: prosthesis reduces bone load → atrophy.
  • 🔵 Endothelial: shear → alignment via PECAM-1/VE-cad/VEGFR2.
  • 🟢 Fibroblasts contract collagen matrix.
  • 🔴 Wolff’s Law: stress influences bone strength.
  • 🟢 Optogenetics: light-sensitive channels control activity.
  • 🔵 Paclitaxel stabilizes MT, inhibits division.

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

AspectActin Filaments (X)Microtubules (Y)
MonomerG-Actinα/β Tubulin
FunctionMotility/ContractionTransport/Division
NucleotideATPGTP
DynamicsTreadmillingDynamic Instability
Motor ProteinMyosinKinesin/Dynein

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Treating cells as simple viscous bags (trap: ignore cytoskeleton).
  • Forgetting GTP/ATP in dynamics.
  • Overlooking stress shielding in implants.

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • Cytoskeleton: “AMI” – Actin (Movement), MT (Transport), IF (Integrity).
  • Actin Ends: “+ Grows, - Goes” (add +, remove -).

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

What is mechanotransduction?

Conversion of physical forces (e.g., shear) into biochemical responses like gene expression via cytoskeleton.

Describe actin polymerization.

G-actin nucleates with ATP, elongates at (+) end, hydrolyzes to ADP, treadmills with disassembly at (-).

Active Recall Bank

  • What sequence in fibronectin binds integrins? (RGD)
  • Name two types of fibronectin. (Soluble plasma, insoluble cellular)
  • Focal adhesions in 2D are ____; in 3D ____. (punctate, fibrillar)
  • Chemotaxis responds to ____ gradients. (chemical)
  • Ligand range: distant via blood? (Endocrine)
  • Central paradigm steps: Initiation, ____, Gene Activation. (Transduction)
  • GPCRs have ____ transmembrane segments. (7)
  • RTKs phosphorylate ____ residues. (tyrosine)
  • Cytoskeleton filaments: Actin, ____, Intermediate. (Microtubules)
  • Treadmilling occurs in ____ filaments. (actin)
  • BMP signaling uses ____ proteins. (Smads)
  • Wnt canonical pathway stabilizes ____. (β-catenin)
  • Stress shielding causes bone ____. (atrophy)
  • Myosin uses ____ for force. (ATP)
  • MTOC organizes ____. (microtubules)
  • RGD stands for --____. (Arg-Gly-Asp)
  • Taxis type for light: ____. (Phototaxis)
  • Translation starts with codon ____. (AUG)
  • Ion channels can be ____-gated or ____-gated. (voltage, ligand)
  • Paclitaxel ____ microtubules. (stabilizes)

15-Minute Mock Exam

  1. Explain the role of RGD in cell adhesion and its TE applications.

    RGD (Arg-Gly-Asp) is the integrin-binding site in fibronectin promoting attachment; in TE, short RGD peptides functionalize scaffolds for cell adhesion without full proteins.

  2. Compare focal adhesions in 2D vs 3D environments, with NPC example.

    2D: punctate, ligand-sensitive; 3D: fibrillar, remodeling-focused; for NPCs, 3D stemness maintenance is insensitive to ligands, prioritizing remodeling.

  3. Describe chemotaxis and its measurement.

    Directed migration to chemical gradients; assessed by polarized morphology and proportion of cells moving toward/away from attractant, depending on concentration and gradient steepness.

  4. Outline the central signal transduction paradigm.

    Initiation: ligand-receptor binding; Transduction: cascade activates nuclear TF; Gene activation: TF binds DNA, leading to transcription, translation, and physiological changes.

  5. How does Wnt canonical pathway work?

    Ligand binds Fz/LRP, stabilizes β-catenin, which enters nucleus and interacts with TCF/Lef for gene transcription in development and cancer.

One-Page Cheat Sheet Summary

SectionKey Points
ECM/AdhesionFibronectin: RGD binds α5β1/αVβ3; Focal: 2D punctate vs 3D fibrillar; NPCs: remodeling > binding.
MigrationCycle: Attach-Contract-Detach; Taxis: Chemo (gradients), Hapto (adhesives); Defects: embryonic issues.
SignalingLigands: Endocrine/Paracrine/Autocrine/Juxtacrine; GFs: VEGF/TGF-β; Paradigm: Bind → Cascade → Gene.
ReceptorsGPCR: 7TM, dissociate G; RTK: Dimer/P-Tyr/Ras-ERK; TGF-β: Ser/Thr/Smads; Wnt: β-catenin/TCF.
TransportHydrophobic: Diffuse (NO/AA); Ion Channels: Voltage/Ligand (Ca2+/Na+); Optogenetics: Light control.
CytoskeletonActin: Polymer/Treadmill/Myosin; MT: Dynamic/GTP/Paclitaxel; Mechano: Forces → Signals (Wolff’s Law).
TE AppsDermal matrix: Cell invasion; Growth factors in scaffolds; Avoid stress shielding in implants.
entry.summary: <h1 id="BME-2104-Tissue-Engineering-–-4"><a href="#BME-2104-Tissue-Engineering-–-4" class="headerlink" title="BME 2104 Tissue Engineering –

entry.title: BME 2104 Tissue Engineering – 3
entry.content:
BME 2104 Tissue Engineering – 3

Exam Overview

  • Top 8 topics that appear every year: Morphogenesis, Embryonic Development Stages, Germ Layers and Tissue Origins, Epithelial-Mesenchymal Transition, Blood Vessel Development, Branching Morphogenesis, Cell Adhesion Molecules, Extracellular Matrix Components
  • Colour code: 🔴 Memorise | 🟢 Understand | 🔵 Apply
  • 每年必考 8 大重點：Morphogenesis、胚胎早期發育、Gastrulation 同三胚層、EMT、血管生成、Branching Morphogenesis、細胞黏附分子、細胞外基質 ECM
  • 顏色標記：🔴 死記 | 🟢 明白原理 | 🔵 要會應用同畫圖

Topic 1 – Morphogenesis 形態發生

Big Picture

形態發生就係由受精卵變成有形有體嘅過程，係大自然百萬年演化出嚟嘅最佳「修補藍圖」，組織工程學要抄佢功課先至做到真正修復。

Must-Know Facts（記晒呢12粒）

  • 字源：Morphology（形態）+ Generation（生成）🔴
  • 定義：Pattern formation + Body plan + 左右對稱（mirror-image bilateral symmetry）→ 成人形態🔴
  • 點解同組織工程有關？因為自然修補機制係經過百萬年優化，抄佢最穩陣🔵
  • 要考慮嘅四樣嘢（OIGM）：
    1. Origin 細胞來源
    2. Interaction 細胞同ECM/鄰居互動
    3. Growth factors 生長因子
    4. Mechanisms 點樣令未分化細胞變專門細胞🔴

Mnemonics（廣東話記憶法）

O-I-G-M → 「愛（O）姨（I）姑（G）媽（M）」＝Origin-Interaction-Growth factors-Mechanisms

Topic 2 – Embryonic Development Stages 胚胎早期發育

Big Picture

受精卵 → 卵裂 → 桑椹胚 → 囊胚 → 著床 → 原腸形成（三胚層出現）

Must-Know Facts

  • Zygote 受精卵 → Cleavage 卵裂（唔長大，只係分多粒細細胞，仍然全能 totipotent）🔴
  • Morula 桑椹胚（8-cell開始壓實 compaction）
  • Blastocyst 囊胚：外層 Trophectoderm（變胎盤），內層 Inner cell mass（變嬰兒）
  • Gastrulation 原腸形成：出現 Primitive streak 原條 + Node（最前嘅 organizer）
  • Human 特有：平面盤狀（planar）+ 羊膜腔 + 卵黃囊

順序記憶（廣東話）

「精（Zygote）→裂（Cleavage）→桑（Morula）→囊（Blastocyst）→條（Primitive streak）→三層」

Topic 3 – Germ Layers 三胚層來源表（死記表）

胚層主要組織（廣東話記憶）
Ectoderm 外胚層皮膚、頭髮、神經系統、眼角膜、牙釉質
Mesoderm 中胚層所有肌肉、骨、軟骨、血管、心臟、腎、皮膚真皮層
Endoderm 內胚層消化道、肺襯裏、肝、胰、甲狀腺、膀胱

記憶法：外中內 → 「外神經、中肌肉、內腸肺」

Topic 4 – Epithelial-Mesenchymal Transition (EMT) 上皮-間質轉化

Big Picture

上皮細胞（成張貼實）↔ 間質細胞（四處走）嘅轉變，係所有組織塑形嘅核心機制

特點Epithelial 上皮Mesenchymal 間質
形態鋪石樣（cobblestone）紡錘形、會爬
極性有頂-底極性無極性
黏附緊黏（E-cadherin）鬆散（N-cadherin）
行為保護、屏障遷移、入侵

5大變化（考試最愛問）：

  1. 細胞分裂方向改變
  2. 細胞形狀變
  3. 細胞遷移
  4. 生長/死亡
  5. 分泌改變

Topic 5 – Blood Vessel Development 血管發育

Big Picture

第三週因為擴散唔夠用，要自己起血管網

方式Vasculogenesis 原位血管發生Angiogenesis 血管新生
發生位置無血管區直接起由已有血管長枝
代表時間早期胚胎之後生長/傷口癒合
考試陷阱初生血管網＝Vasculogenesis之後分支＝Angiogenesis

Lumen 形成兩大模型：

  1. Cell hollowing（細胞自己挖空）
  2. Cord hollowing（細胞排隊再拆黏附）← 近年證據多數支持呢個

Topic 6 – Branching Morphogenesis 分支形態發生（肺/腎常見）

Big Picture

FGF10 誘導 + Shh/BMP4 抑制 → 形成分支
最新解釋：Turing 反應-擴散模型（activator BMP-2 + inhibitor MGP）

三種肺分支模式：

  1. Domain branching（低抑制物消耗）
  2. Orthogonal branching（高抑制物消耗）
  3. Planar branching（邊界效應）

Topic 7 – Left-Right Asymmetry 左右不對稱

Big Picture

點解心臟喺左邊？唔係純粹隨機！

兩大機制：

  1. Node 纖毛左掃流 → 形態素梯度
  2. 單細胞手性（chirality）← 連雙胞胎都可能相反，證明好早期已有

Topic 8 – Cell Adhesion Molecules 細胞黏附分子（死記表）

分子主要功能例子需唔需要 Calcium
Cadherins細胞-細胞（同型）E-cadherin（上皮）要
Integrins細胞-基質（雙向信號）αvβ3唔要
Selectins白血球滾動P/E/L-selectin要
Ig-CAMs細胞-細胞（異型都得）N-CAM唔要

Topic 9 – Extracellular Matrix (ECM) 細胞外基質

死記成分表

  • Collagen I（強度，最多）
  • Elastin（彈性，肺/血管/皮膚）
  • Laminin（基底膜網絡，維持幹細胞）
  • Fibronectin（黏integrin，兩種：血漿型/細胞型）
  • Type IV Collagen（基底膜專用，形成網）

基底膜 vs 基底板：
Basement membrane：
Basal lamina（60-120nm）+ Type VI collagen + 網狀纖維 = Basement membrane

Active Recall Bank（20條快問快答，遮住答案試背）

  1. Morphogenesis 中文？ → 形態發生
  2. 三胚層順序？ → 外-中-內
  3. 肌肉同血管嚟自邊層？ → 中胚層
  4. EMT 5大變化？ → 分裂方向、形狀、遷移、生死、分泌
  5. Vasculogenesis 同 Angiogenesis 分別？ → 原位 vs 由已有血管生
  6. Lumen 形成主流模型？ → Cord hollowing
  7. Branching 嘅 activator 同 inhibitor？ → BMP-2 + MGP
  8. 左右不對稱最早期機制？ → 單細胞手性 chirality
  9. E-cadherin 屬邊類黏附分子？ → Cadherin
  10. Integrin 係咪要 Calcium？ → 唔要
    …（其餘同上）

15-Minute Mock Exam（中英對照）

  1. 用三句話解釋點解組織工程要學 Morphogenesis。

    因為自然嘅修補機制係經過百萬年演化出嚟最優化，組織工程抄佢功課先做到真正再生。

  2. 由受精卵到三胚層嘅正確順序係咩？

    Zygote → Cleavage → Morula → Blastocyst → Primitive streak → Gastrulation → 三胚層

  3. 腎同血管嚟自邊個胚層？

    中胚層 Mesoderm

One-Page Cheat Sheet（中英對照）

英文關鍵詞中文考試必記一句
Morphogenesis形態發生自然修補藍圖
Gastrulation原腸形成出現 primitive streak + 3層
Ectoderm外胚層皮膚＋神經
Mesoderm中胚層肌肉＋骨＋血管
Endoderm內胚層腸肺肝胰
EMT上皮-間質轉化細胞變「爬」模式
Vasculogenesis原位血管發生早期胚胎
Angiogenesis血管新生之後分支
Cadherin鈣黏蛋白細胞-細胞，要Ca²⁺
Integrin整聯蛋白細胞-基質，雙向信號

Topic 1 – Morphogenesis

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Morphogenesis is the developmental process shaping pattern formation, body plan, and symmetry in organisms, crucial for tissue engineering to mimic natural repair.
Mind map: Morphogenesis → Definition (Morphology + Generation) → Cascade (Pattern Formation → Body Plan → Bilateral Symmetry → Adult Form) → Importance (Natural Selection → Optimal Function → Tissue Repair Mechanisms)

Must-Know Facts (max 12 bullet points)

  • Derived from “morphology” and “generation” 🔴
  • Involves pattern formation, body plan establishment, and mirror-image symmetry in musculoskeletal structures 🔴
  • Key for tissue engineering as it incorporates natural repair mechanisms for damaged tissues 🟢
  • Considers cell origins for organ formation 🟢
  • Focuses on cell-environment interactions (ECM and neighboring cells) 🔵
  • Involves growth factors and their interrelationships in organ formation 🟢
  • Examines mechanisms inducing undifferentiated precursors to organ-specific cells 🔵
  • Result of natural selection over millions of years for optimal adaptation 🟢
  • Essential for repairing non-self-repairing tissues 🔵
  • Human embryo adopts planar morphology during gastrulation 🟢
  • Primitive streak forms via delamination of mesodermal cells 🔴
  • Nodal signaling creates morphogenic gradients 🟢

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

AspectEpithelial Cells (X)Mesenchymal Cells (Y)
ArrangementIn sheets/tubes, tightly connectedUnconnected, independent units
FunctionProtection, shape maintenanceMigration, individual operation
PolarityApical-basal polarityLack of polarity
AdhesionStrong cell-cell (cadherins)Loose, via cadherins/ECM

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Confusing morphogenesis with simple cell division; trap: Questions asking to differentiate from cleavage (morphogenesis involves pattern and symmetry, not just mass increase)
  • Overlooking importance in tissue engineering; mistake: Assuming it’s only embryonic, ignore repair applications
  • Misidentifying morphogenic gradients as uniform; trap: Diagrams without distance-dependent concentration

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • MORPH: Millions Of Years Refine Patterns Holistically (for natural selection importance)
  • Things to Consider: O-I-G-M (Origin of cells, Interaction with environment, Growth factors, Mechanisms of specialization)

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

What is morphogenesis and why is it important in tissue engineering?

Morphogenesis is the developmental cascade of pattern formation, body plan establishment, and bilateral symmetry culminating in adult form; it’s vital as tissue formation results from natural selection for optimal function, aiding incorporation of repair mechanisms in engineering non-self-repairing tissues.

Explain nodal signaling in embryonic disk.

Nodal (BMP4 family) is expressed proximally in epiblast, localizes to node, creating a descending gradient where highest levels are at node, decreasing with distance to form morphogenic gradient.

Topic 2 – Embryonic Development Stages

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Embryonic development progresses from zygote to gastrulation, establishing germ layers for organ formation.
Mind map: Zygote → Cleavage (Blastomeres → Morula) → Compaction → Blastocyst (Trophectoderm + Inner Cell Mass) → Gastrulation (Primitive Streak → Germ Layers: Ectoderm/Mesoderm/Endoderm)

Must-Know Facts (max 12 bullet points)

  • Zygote: Fertilized oocyte 🔴
  • Cleavage: Divisions without growth, increases cell number, blastomeres totipotent 🟢
  • Morula: 8-cell stage, first differentiation, compaction 🔴
  • Blastocyst: Forms prior to implantation, inner cell mass becomes epiblast 🟢
  • Gastrulation: Establishes 3 germ layers (ectoderm, endoderm, mesoderm) 🔵
  • Human embryo: Planar morphology, amniotic cavity, yolk sac 🔴
  • Primitive endoderm proliferates to form yolk sac 🟢
  • Extraembryonic membranes: Amnion (fluid environment), yolk sac (RBC formation) 🟢
  • Implantation in uterine wall after blastocyst stage 🔴
  • First differentiation at morula stage 🔵
  • Trophectoderm mediates implantation and placenta formation 🟢
  • Umbilical cord connects embryo to placenta 🔴

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

StagePre-Gastrulation (X)Gastrulation (Y)
StructureZygote to blastocyst, sphericalPlanar disk, primitive streak
DifferentiationTotipotent to pluripotent3 germ layers established
Key EventCleavage, compactionSpecification of ecto/endo/meso
OutcomeImplantation prepAdult organ precursors

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Mixing morula and blastocyst; trap: Questions on cell potency (morula blastomeres totipotent, blastocyst pluripotent)
  • Forgetting extraembryonic roles; mistake: Assuming all structures embryonic
  • Incorrect sequence; trap: Timeline diagrams with wrong order

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • Z-C-M-B-G: Zygote-Cleavage-Morula-Blastocyst-Gastrulation
  • Germ Layers: Every Monday Evening (Ectoderm-Mesoderm-Endoderm)

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

Describe cleavage and its outcome.

Cleavage: Zygote divisions without growth, producing totipotent blastomeres; increases cell number without mass, leading to morula.

What occurs during gastrulation?

Subsequent steps establish proper structure for 3 germ layers (ectoderm, endoderm, mesoderm) via primitive streak formation.

Topic 3 – Germ Layers and Tissue Origins

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Germ layers (ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm) originate during gastrulation and give rise to specific tissues and organs.
Mind map: Gastrulation → Ectoderm (Skin/Nervous System) → Mesoderm (Muscles/Connective Tissue) → Endoderm (Digestive/Respiratory Organs)

Must-Know Facts (max 12 bullet points)

  • Ectoderm: Skin epidermis, hair, nails, sweat glands, nervous system, retina, lens, cornea, inner ear 🔴
  • Mesoderm: All muscles, dermis, connective tissue, bone, cartilage, blood vessels, kidneys, reproductive organs 🟢
  • Endoderm: Lining of digestive tract, trachea, bronchi, lungs, liver, pancreas, thyroid, parathyroid, thymus, urinary bladder 🔴
  • Origin from primitive streak and node during gastrulation 🟢
  • Node as organizing center 🔵
  • Morphogenic gradient via Nodal signaling 🟢
  • Primitive streak thickens ectoderm midline via mesoderm delamination 🔴
  • Three definitive germ layers construct adult organism 🟢
  • Trophoblast contiguous with ectoderm as trophectoderm 🔴
  • Yolk sac from primitive endoderm 🟢
  • Amniotic cavity between epiblast and trophoblast 🔴
  • Human development video illustrates stages 🟢

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

Germ LayerEctoderm (X)Endoderm (Y)
OriginOuter layerInner layer
TissuesSkin, nerves, eyesDigestive tract, lungs, glands
ExamplesBrain, spinal cordLiver, pancreas
FunctionProtection, sensationAbsorption, secretion

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Misassigning tissues; trap: Kidney from endoderm (actually mesoderm)
  • Confusing primitive vs definitive layers; mistake: Primitive endoderm as definitive
  • Ignoring mesoderm’s broad role; trap: Questions on blood vessels (mesoderm)

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • Ecto: External (Skin/Eyes/Nerves)
  • Meso: Middle (Muscles/Bones/Blood)
  • Endo: Internal (Guts/Glands/Lungs)

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

List tissues from mesoderm.

All muscles, dermis of skin, connective tissue, bone, cartilage, blood vessels, kidneys, reproductive organs.

What is the role of the node in germ layer formation?

Anterior-most tip of primitive streak, functions as organizing center for morphogenic gradients.

Topic 4 – Epithelial-Mesenchymal Transition (EMT)

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

EMT/MET involves cellular changes in division, shape, migration, growth, and secretion for tissue morphogenesis.
Mind map: EMT → Loss of Cell-Cell Adhesion → Gain Cell-ECM Adhesion → Polarity Change → Motility/Invasion → Examples (Condensation/Shape Change)

Must-Know Facts (max 12 bullet points)

  • Epithelial: Sheets/tubes, tightly connected, polarity 🔴
  • Mesenchymal: Unconnected, independent, no polarity 🟢
  • EMT accompanies morphogenesis with changes in division direction/number 🔵
  • Cell shape changes (e.g., cobblestone to acini in MCF-10A) 🟢
  • Cell migration/movement 🔴
  • Cell growth/death 🟢
  • Secretion changes 🔵
  • Example: Vascular mesenchymal cell aggregation (condensation) 🔴
  • MCF-10A on Matrigel forms acini 🟢
  • Lumen formation via cell death in acini 🔴
  • Polarity dependent on cadherins 🟢
  • Occurs in wound healing for alignment 🔵

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

ProcessEMT (X)MET (Y)
ChangeEpithelial to mesenchymalMesenchymal to epithelial
AdhesionLose cell-cell, gain cell-ECMGain cell-cell, lose cell-ECM
PolarityLose apical-basalGain apical-basal
ExampleWound healing invasionTissue stabilization

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Confusing EMT with apoptosis; trap: Lumen formation questions (via death, not just hollowing)
  • Overlooking examples; mistake: Assuming all cells undergo EMT equally
  • Misidentifying polarity role; trap: Cadherin dependency in alignment

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • EMT: Every Migration Transforms (Division/Shape/Movement/Growth/Secretion)
  • 5 Changes: Di-Sha-Mi-Gro-Se (Direction/Shape/Migration/Growth/Secretion)

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

Describe EMT changes.

  1. Direction/number of divisions; 2. Shape changes; 3. Movement/migration; 4. Growth/death; 5. Membrane/secretion changes.

Explain MCF-10A example.

At confluence, cobblestone; in 3D Matrigel, proliferate to clusters then acini with polarized outer layer and inner cell death for lumen.

Topic 5 – Blood Vessel Development

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Blood vessels form via vasculogenesis and angiogenesis to meet embryonic needs by third week.
Mind map: Vasculogenesis (In Situ Endothelial Formation → Network → Stabilization) → Angiogenesis (Sprouting from Existing) → Structure (Adventitia/Media/Intima) → Lumen (Hollowing/Cord)

Must-Know Facts (max 12 bullet points)

  • Starts by third gestational week due to diffusion limits 🔴
  • Two processes: Vasculogenesis (in situ in non-vascularized tissue) 🟢
  • Angiogenesis (new vessels from existing) 🔵
  • Vessel walls: Adventitia, media, intima (capillaries thin endothelium) 🟢
  • Linked to heart development 🔴
  • Initial network: Veins/arteries + plexus 🟢
  • Growth factors stabilize walls, recruit pericytes/SMCs 🔵
  • Blood flow essential for proper development 🔴
  • Endothelial tube on fibronectin lines (30μm wide) 🟢
  • Lumen models: Cell hollowing (vacuoles coalesce) or cord hollowing (shape changes) 🔴
  • Cord hollowing more feasible for vasculogenesis 🟢
  • Luminal cell death alternative mechanism 🔵

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

ProcessVasculogenesis (X)Angiogenesis (Y)
LocationNon-vascularized tissueFrom existing vessels
MechanismIn situ endothelial formationSprouting/branching
StageEarly embryo, linked to heartLater growth/remodeling
OutcomeInitial network (veins/arteries/plexus)Expanded vasculature

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Confusing vasculogenesis with angiogenesis; trap: Initial vs expansion questions
  • Ignoring flow role; mistake: Assuming vessels develop without hemodynamics
  • Wrong lumen model; trap: Assuming only hollowing (evidence for cord)

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • V-A: Vasculogenesis (Virgin Areas) vs Angiogenesis (Add to Existing)
  • Lumen: Hollow Cords Vacate (Hollowing/Cord/Vacuoles)

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

Differentiate vasculogenesis and angiogenesis.

Vasculogenesis: In situ endothelial formation in non-vascularized tissue, initial network; Angiogenesis: New endothelium from existing vessels.

Describe lumen formation models.

Cell hollowing: Vacuoles coalesce and interconnect; Cord hollowing: Packed cells change shape, break junctions for extracellular lumen.

Topic 6 – Branching Morphogenesis

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Branching morphogenesis creates complex structures like lungs via inductive chemicals and inhibitors.
Mind map: Traditional Model (FGF10 Induce → Shh/BMP4 Inhibit) → Types (Domain/Orthogonal/Planar) → Reaction-Diffusion (Activator/Inhibitor → Patterns)

Must-Know Facts (max 12 bullet points)

  • Traditional: FGF10 inductive, Shh/BMP4/Sprouty inhibit for branching 🔴
  • Lung types: Domain, orthogonal, planar 🟢
  • Reaction-diffusion: Turing’s activator-inhibitor system 🔵
  • Activator: BMP-2 (postulated); Inhibitor: MGP 🟢
  • Equations involve diffusion, reaction, chemotaxis 🔴
  • Applications: Pigment distribution, hair follicles 🟢
  • Domain branching: Low inhibitor consumption 🔵
  • Orthogonal: High consumption 🔴
  • Planar: With boundary effect 🟢
  • Simulation matches lung anatomy 🔵
  • Alan Turing: Mathematical biology pioneer 🔴
  • System: Pair activator-inhibitor with cells 🟢

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

TypeDomain Branching (X)Orthogonal Branching (Y)
Inhibitor ConsumptionLowHigh
MorphologyAlong surfacePerpendicular rotation
SimulationMatches early lungMatches mid-lung
SwitchN/ATo planar with boundary

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Ignoring Turing’s role; trap: History questions on models
  • Misapplying equations; mistake: Forgetting cell term
  • Confusing branch types; trap: Lung anatomy diagrams

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • Branch Types: DOP (Domain-Orthogonal-Planar)
  • Turing: Turing’s Universal Reaction Inhibits Natural Growth (for activator-inhibitor)

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

Explain traditional branching model.

FGF10 as inductive chemical; Shh, BMP4, Sprouty inhibit FGF10, leading to localized branching.

Describe reaction-diffusion in branching.

Activator (BMP-2) and inhibitor (MGP) system with cells; diffusion/reaction create patterns like lung branches.

Topic 7 – Left-Right Asymmetry

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Left-right asymmetry arises from cellular chirality and cilia to establish organ sidedness.
Mind map: LR Asymmetry → Cilia (Node Flow → Gradient) → Planar Chirality (Epithelium → Rotation) → Examples (Heart/Fiber → Twins/Mirror Imaging)

Must-Know Facts (max 12 bullet points)

  • Prominent from microscopic to macroscopic levels 🔴
  • Cilia at node generate leftward flow for sidedness 🟢
  • Flow sets morphogen gradient or activation 🔵
  • Planar cell polarity orients cilia 🔴
  • Not solely cilia; early twins show mirror imaging 🟢
  • Single cell chirality: BDM induces counter-clockwise torsion 🔵
  • Hindgut rotation driven by epithelial planar chirality 🔴
  • Vascular mesenchymal cells form chiral structures on patterns 🟢
  • Skeletal myogenesis: Chiral orientation before fusion 🔵
  • Myotubes on wide grooves show intense contractions 🔴
  • Engineering: Actin striation in myotubes 🟢
  • Twins: Book-ending defects suggest pre-cilia chirality 🔵

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

MechanismCilia Flow (X)Cell Chirality (Y)
LevelMulticellular (node)Single cell
ExampleEmbryo-wide asymmetryZygote torsion, hindgut
DependencyFluid flowIntrinsic polarity
EvidenceScience mag diagramsBDM treatment

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Assuming only cilia; trap: Twin questions (not 100% identical)
  • Overlooking single cell role; mistake: All asymmetry post-gastrulation
  • Confusing torsion direction; trap: BDM timing effects

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • LR: Leftward Rotation (Cilia flow)
  • Chirality: Cells Have Intrinsic Rotation Always Leading Inward (for torsion)

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

How do cilia establish LR asymmetry?

Node cilia generate leftward fluid flow, setting extracellular morphogen gradient or differential activation.

Explain single cell chirality in asymmetry.

BDM treatment post-fertilization induces counter-clockwise torsion in zygotes, contributing to LR before cilia.

Topic 8 – Cell Adhesion Molecules

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

Cell adhesion via CAMs like cadherins, integrins, selectins mediates cell-cell/matrix interactions for tissue formation.
Mind map: Adhesion → Families (Cadherins → Integrins → Selectins → Ig CAMs) → Types (Homotypic/Heterotypic) → Functions (Junctions/Signaling/Rolling)

Must-Know Facts (max 12 bullet points)

  • Four families: Cadherins (Ca-dependent, cell-cell), Ig CAMs (Ca-independent), Integrins (cell-matrix), Selectins (leukocyte rolling) 🔴
  • Homotypic: Same molecules; Heterotypic: Different 🟢
  • Cadherins: E (epithelial), N (neural), P (placenta); Ca-binding domain 🔵
  • Integrins: αβ heterodimers, 18α/8β, mechanotransducers, bidirectional signaling 🔴
  • Selectins: L (leukocytes), E (ECs), P (platelets/ECs); Ca-dependent carbohydrate-binding 🟢
  • Focal adhesions: Concentrated sites via integrins 🔵
  • In wound healing: Cadherin-dependent polarity 🔴
  • Leukocyte rolling: Selectin-mediated transient adhesion 🟢
  • Extravasation: Selectin arrest + integrin adhesion 🔵
  • Lung-on-chip: Reconstitutes alveolar-capillary interface 🔴
  • Inflammation: Neutrophil extravasation via cytokines/bacteria 🟢
  • Cell-cell vs cell-substrate adhesion crucial for engineering 🔵

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

MoleculeCadherins (X)Integrins (Y)
BindingCell-cell, Ca-dependentCell-matrix, bidirectional
StructureTransmembrane, homotypicαβ heterodimer, tails ~50aa
ExamplesE-cadherin (epithelium)α1β1 (collagen)
FunctionJunctions, polarityFocal adhesions, signaling

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Confusing cadherins with integrins; trap: Binding types (cell-cell vs matrix)
  • Forgetting Ca role; mistake: Cadherins work without Ca (dimer fails)
  • Misunderstanding selectin rolling; trap: Permanent vs transient adhesion

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • CAM Families: C-I-S-I (Cadherins-Integrins-Selectins-Ig)
  • Integrin: Inside-Out, Outside-In (Bidirectional signaling)

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

Compare cadherins and integrins.

Cadherins: Ca-dependent cell-cell adhesion, homotypic, e.g., E-cadherin; Integrins: Cell-matrix, αβ, mechanotransducers, bidirectional.

Describe selectin-mediated rolling.

Transient adhesion under flow; L-selectin on leukocytes binds PSGL-1 on ECs, leading to arrest then integrin-mediated transmigration.

Topic 9 – Extracellular Matrix (ECM)

Big Picture (1 sentence + mind map in text)

ECM provides structural support and signaling via proteins, GAGs, and glycoproteins surrounding cells.
Mind map: ECM → Components (Proteins → GAGs → Glycoproteins) → Basal Lamina (Sheet 60-120nm) → Types (Collagen/Laminin/Elastin/Fibronectin)

Must-Know Facts (max 12 bullet points)

  • 3D network of fibers in hydrated gel 🔴
  • Secreted locally by cells, assembles scaffold for attachment/proliferation 🟢
  • Cells modify via enzymes/load 🔵
  • Components: Proteins (collagen/elastin/laminin), GAGs (chondroitin/heparin), Proteoglycans (fibrinogen) 🔴
  • Basal lamina: 60-120nm sheet, surrounds epithelia, important in repair/embryogenesis 🟢
  • Basement membrane: Basal lamina + collagen VI + reticular tissue 🔵
  • Collagen: Main strength, 25-35% body protein, triple helix (Type I: 2α1+1α2) 🔴
  • Type IV: In basal lamina, 7S/middle/NC1 domains, forms hexamers/tetramers 🟢
  • Laminin: Networks in basal lamina, binds cells/integrins, for stem cell culture 🔵
  • Elastin: Elasticity, stretches 5x, interwoven with collagen 🔴
  • Fibronectin: Binds integrins/collagen/fibrin, soluble (plasma) or insoluble (cellular) 🟢
  • Wrinkle: Broken elastin replaced by collagen, looser/stiffer skin 🔵

Key Formulas / Diagrams (use code blocks)

Comparison Table (“X vs Y” in proper Markdown table)

ComponentCollagen (X)Elastin (Y)
FunctionStrength, rigidityElasticity, stretch
StructureTriple helix fibrilsHydrophobic, 750aa
LocationTendons, ligamentsLung, vessels, bladder
In AgingMaintains integrityDegradation causes wrinkles

Common Mistakes & Examiner Traps

  • Confusing basal lamina with basement; trap: Thickness/composition (lamina 60-120nm, basement includes collagen layer)
  • Misidentifying collagen types; mistake: Type IV as fibrillar (actually network-forming)
  • Overlooking fibronectin types; trap: Soluble vs insoluble roles

Mnemonics / Memory Hooks

  • ECM: Extra Cellular Material (Proteins-GAGs-Glycoproteins)
  • Collagen Types: I Fibril, IV Basal (1-Fiber, 4-Foundation)

Past Exam Questions + Model Answers (use > blockquote for answers)

What is ECM composition?

Proteins (collagen, elastin, laminin), GAGs (chondroitin, heparin), glycoproteins/proteoglycans (fibronectin, fibrinogen).

Describe Type IV collagen structure.

Alpha-chains with 7S, triple-helical, NC1 domains; two assemble to NC1 hexamer, four to 7S tetramer.

Active Recall Bank

  • What is morphogenesis? _____
  • Define cleavage. _____
  • Name the 3 germ layers. _____
  • What does ectoderm form? _____
  • Describe EMT. _____
  • Differentiate vasculogenesis from angiogenesis. _____
  • What is reaction-diffusion mechanism? _____
  • How do cilia contribute to LR asymmetry? _____
  • Name 4 CAM families. _____
  • What is integrin function? _____
  • Define basal lamina. _____
  • Main ECM component for strength? _____
  • Laminin role in basal lamina? _____
  • Elastin vs collagen in skin? _____
  • Fibronectin types? _____
  • Primitive streak formation? _____
  • Nodal signaling gradient? _____
  • Example of cell shape change? _____
  • Lumen formation models? _____
  • Branching types in lung? _____

15-Minute Mock Exam

  1. Explain the importance of morphogenesis in tissue engineering.

    It’s a process from natural selection for optimal tissue function; incorporating mechanisms aids repair of non-self-repairing tissues.

  2. Describe gastrulation process.

    Establishes 3 germ layers (ecto/endo/meso) via primitive streak in planar embryo with amniotic cavity and yolk sac.

  3. Compare epithelial and mesenchymal cells.

    Epithelial: Tightly connected sheets, polar; Mesenchymal: Loose independent units, non-polar.

  4. What are the two lumen formation models?

    Cell hollowing (vacuoles coalesce); Cord hollowing (shape changes break junctions).

  5. Explain reaction-diffusion in branching.

    Activator-inhibitor pair (e.g., BMP-2/MGP) with diffusion/reaction creates patterns like branches.

  6. How is LR asymmetry established beyond cilia?

    Single cell chirality (torsion) and planar chirality (epithelium rotation) contribute, as seen in twins.

  7. Differentiate cadherins and selectins.

    Cadherins: Ca-dependent cell-cell junctions; Selectins: Ca-dependent carbohydrate-binding for leukocyte rolling.

  8. What is ECM and its components?

    Non-cellular network for support/signaling; proteins (collagen), GAGs, glycoproteins (fibronectin).

One-Page Cheat Sheet Summary

TopicKey Points
MorphogenesisPattern/body plan/symmetry; Importance: Repair mechanisms
Embryonic StagesZygote → Cleavage → Morula → Blastocyst → Gastrulation
Germ LayersEcto: Skin/nerves; Meso: Muscles/bones; Endo: Guts/glands
EMTEpithelial → Mesenchymal: Shape/migration changes
Blood VesselsVasculogenesis (in situ); Angiogenesis (sprout); Lumen: Hollow/cord
BranchingFGF10 induce; Types: Domain/Orthogonal/Planar; Turing RD
LR AsymmetryCilia flow; Cell chirality: Torsion/rotation
AdhesionCadherins (cell-cell); Integrins (matrix); Selectins (rolling)
ECMCollagen (strength); Laminin (networks); Elastin (stretch); Fibronectin (binding)

entry.title: Biomaterials-Outline | 生物材料-大綱
entry.content:
Biomaterials-Outline | 生物材料-大綱

Lecture 1: History, Definition, and Outline | 第一講：歷史、定義與大綱

  • Topics Covered | 涵蓋主題 :
    • Definition of Biomaterials | 生物材料的定義
    • Characteristics of four generations | 四個世代的特徵
    • Major market shares | 主要市場份額
    • Requirements of biomaterials | 生物材料的要求
    • Major types and their pros and cons | 主要類型及其優缺點
    • Forces in biomaterials | 生物材料中的作用力
    • Three aspects in biomaterials | 生物材料的三個方面
    • Restenosis | 再狹窄
    • Need of selection and processing of biomaterials | 生物材料選擇與加工的需求

Lecture 2: Structure and Properties of Biomaterials | 第二講：生物材料的結構與性質

  • Topics Covered | 涵蓋主題:
    • Atomic Structure and Bonding | 原子結構與鍵結
    • Crystalline and Amorphous Structures | 晶體與非晶體結構
    • Mechanical Properties | 機械性質
    • Thermal Properties | 熱性質
    • Electrical Properties | 電性質
    • Optical Properties | 光學性質
    • Magnetic Properties | 磁性質
    • Surface Properties and Modifications | 表面性質與改性

Lecture 3: Polymers and Hydrogels | 聚合物和水凝膠

  • Topics Covered | 涵蓋主題:
    • Definition of Polymer | 聚合物的定義
    • Equilibrium Swelling Theory | 平衡膨脹理論
    • Calculation of Swelling Ratio | 膨脹比率的計算
    • Mesh Size | 網格尺寸
    • Chemical and Physical Hydrogels; Their Difference in Swelling Ratio and Mesh Size and Strength | 化學與物理水凝膠；其膨脹比率、網格尺寸和強度的差異
    • Lower Critical Solution Temperature | 較低臨界溶液溫度
    • Advantages of Hydrogel for Biomedical Applications | 水凝膠在生物醫學應用中的優勢
    • Representative Bioapplications of Hydrogel | 水凝膠的代表性生物應用
    • Degradation of Hydrogel | 水凝膠的降解

Lecture 4: Ceramics and Metals | 陶瓷與金屬

  • Topics Covered | 涵蓋主題:
    • Advantages of ceramics and metals for medical implants | 醫用植入物中陶瓷和金屬的優勢
    • Composition of Bone | 骨的組成
    • Three types of ceramics in biomaterials and their differences | 生物材料中三種陶瓷及其區別
    • Difference between biocompatibility, bioactivity and biodegradability | 生物相容性、生物活性和生物可降解性的區別
    • Hydroxyapatite and its properties and applications | 羥基磷灰石的性質與應用
    • Solid state sintering | 固態燒結
    • Basic properties of metals | 金屬的基本性質
    • Relative elastic modulus of different biomaterials | 不同生物材料的彈性模量比較
    • Criteria for orthopaedic implant and choices of materials | 骨科植入物的標準及材料選擇
    • Comparison of three types of metals as orthopaedic implant | 三種金屬作為骨科植入物的比較
    • Corrosion of metallic implants and solutions | 金屬植入物的腐蝕及解決方案

Lecture 5: Micro/nano-sized biomaterials | 微/奈米尺寸生物材料

  • Topics Covered | 涵蓋主題:
    • Nanomaterials | 奈米材料
      • Gold Nanoparticles (AuNPs) | 金奈米粒子
      • Quantum Dots (QDs) | 量子點
      • Liposomes & Lipid Nanoparticles (LNPs) | 脂質體與脂質奈米粒子
    • Fabrication Techniques | 製造技術
      • Synthesis Methods | 合成方法
      • Surface Engineering | 表面工程
    • Biomedical Applications | 生物醫學應用
      • Principles of Nanomedicine | 奈米醫學原理
      • Gene Therapy & Delivery | 基因療法與傳遞

Lecture 6: Host reaction to biomaterials and their evaluation | 生物材料的宿主反應及其評估

  • Topics Covered | 涵蓋主題:
    • Tissue or host responses to injury:
      • Implant material-tissue interaction | 植入材料-組織相互作用
      • Coagulation | 凝血
      • Inflammation | 發炎
      • Angiogenesis | 血管生成
      • Granulation tissue formation | 肉芽組織形成
      • Foreign body reaction | 異物反應
      • Tissue fibrosis | 組織纖維化
      • Wound remodeling | 傷口重塑
    • Biofilms:
      • Bacterial in biofilm | 生物膜中的細菌
      • Biofilm development | 生物膜發展
      • Device/implant related infection | 器械/植入物相關感染
      • Infection detection | 感染檢測
      • Prevention and treatment of infection | 感染的預防與治療
